# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions test definitional knowledge, basic Venn diagram interpretation, and direct application of identities. Answers should be **precise, formula-driven, and concise** (≈80–120 words).

---

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a Set. With a suitable example, distinguish between a *subset* and a *proper subset*.**

**Model Answer:**

A **set** is a well-defined collection of distinct objects, considered as an object itself. "Well-defined" means that for any object $x$, we can unambiguously determine whether $x$ is a member of the set.

**Subset:** $A \subseteq B$ if every element of $A$ is also in $B$. Formally:

$$A \subseteq B \iff \forall x, (x \in A \Rightarrow x \in B)$$

**Proper Subset:** $A \subset B$ if $A \subseteq B$ **and** $A \neq B$ (i.e., $B$ contains at least one element not in $A$).

**Example:** Let $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $B = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Then $A \subseteq B$ is true, and $A \subset B$ is also true because $4, 5 \in B$ but $4, 5 \notin A$. However, $A \subseteq A$ is true, but $A \subset A$ is **false** (a set is never a proper subset of itself).

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State and prove De Morgan's Law for two sets.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For any two sets $A$ and $B$ within a universal set $U$:

$$(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$$

**Proof (using set-builder notation and logical equivalences):**

$$(A \cup B)^c = \{x \in U \mid x \notin (A \cup B)\}$$

$$= \{x \in U \mid \neg(x \in A \lor x \in B)\}$$

Applying logical De Morgan's law:

$$= \{x \in U \mid (x \notin A) \land (x \notin B)\}$$

$$= \{x \in U \mid x \in A^c \text{ AND } x \in B^c\}$$

$$= A^c \cap B^c \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification via Venn Diagram:** Shading the region outside $A \cup B$ in a Venn diagram visually yields the same region as the intersection of the regions outside $A$ and outside $B$, confirming the identity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-level Part B questions are **compulsory** from the registered module and offer an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Each question typically contains sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks, and may include Venn-diagram-based derivation OR a real-world application problem. Always show *all* steps, define *all* sets, and end with a boxed answer.

---

### ⭐ Question B1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Set Theory & Inclusion-Exclusion

**Q:** In a clinical trial screening 500 patients for three comorbidities — **Hypertension (H)**, **Diabetes (D)**, and **Cardiac Arrest (C)** — the following data was collected:

- 220 patients have H
- 180 patients have D
- 150 patients have C
- 80 have both H and D
- 60 have both H and C
- 50 have both D and C
- 30 have all three conditions

**(i)** Draw a Venn diagram and label all seven regions.
**(ii)** Find the number of patients with **at least one** of the three conditions.
**(iii)** Find the number of patients with **none** of the three conditions.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Venn Diagram with Seven Labeled Regions:**

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  Universal Set U: All 500 Patients                  │
        │                                                     │
        │      ╭─────────╮         ╭─────────╮                │
        │     │  H only  │ H∩D only │  D only  │               │
        │     │   110    │    50    │   100    │               │
        │      ╰─────────┤         ├─────────╯                │
        │                ╭─────────╮                          │
        │               │ H∩D∩C =  │                          │
        │                │    30    │                          │
        │                ╰─────────╯                          │
        │     ... (similar structure for C)                   │
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Region Calculation (using inclusion-exclusion logic):**

- **Triple intersection (H ∩ D ∩ C):** 30
- **H and D only:** $\vert H \cap D \vert - \vert H \cap D \cap C \vert = 80 - 30 = 50$
- **H and C only:** $60 - 30 = 30$
- **D and C only:** $50 - 30 = 20$
- **H only:** $220 - 50 - 30 - 30 = 110$
- **D only:** $180 - 50 - 20 - 30 = 80$
- **C only:** $150 - 30 - 20 - 30 = 70$

**(ii) Patients with at least one condition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert H \cup D \cup C \vert &= 220 + 180 + 150 - 80 - 60 - 50 + 30 \\
&= 550 - 190 + 30 \\
&= 390
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Patients with none of the conditions:**

$$\vert (H \cup D \cup C)^c \vert = \vert U \vert - 390 = 500 - 390 = \boxed{110}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via Region Sum:** $110 + 80 + 70 + 50 + 30 + 20 + 30 = 390$ ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Power Set, Cartesian Product, and Medical Application

**Q:** Let $A = \{\text{Fever}, \text{Cough}, \text{Fatigue}\}$ represent three symptoms tracked in a diagnostic expert system.

**(i)** Find $\vert \mathcal{P}(A) \vert$ and list all subsets of size 2.
**(ii)** If $B = \{\text{Viral}, \text{Bacterial}\}$ represents possible infection types, find $\vert A \times B \vert$ and explain its medical informatics significance.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Power Set Cardinality:**

$$\vert \mathcal{P}(A) \vert = 2^{\vert A \vert} = 2^3 = 8$$

The 8 subsets are:

$$\mathcal{P}(A) = \{\emptyset, \{\text{F}\}, \{\text{C}\}, \{\text{Fg}\}, \{\text{F,C}\}, \{\text{F,Fg}\}, \{\text{C,Fg}\}, \{\text{F,C,Fg}\}\}$$

Number of subsets of size 2:

$$\binom{3}{2} = \frac{3!}{2! \cdot 1!} = 3$$

The three 2-element subsets are:

$$\{\text{Fever, Cough}\}, \quad \{\text{Fever, Fatigue}\}, \quad \{\text{Cough, Fatigue}\}$$

**(ii) Cartesian Product:**

$$\vert A \times B \vert = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert = 3 \times 2 = 6$$

The six ordered pairs are:

$$A \times B = \begin{cases} (\text{Fever, Viral}), & (\text{Fever, Bacterial}), \\ (\text{Cough, Viral}), & (\text{Cough, Bacterial}), \\ (\text{Fatigue, Viral}), & (\text{Fatigue, Bacterial}) \end{cases}$$

**Medical Informatics Significance:** The Cartesian product $A \times B$ enumerates **all possible (symptom, pathogen-type) combinations** that a rule-based diagnostic engine must evaluate. This represents the *hypothesis space* of the diagnostic system. In machine learning, this Cartesian product forms the basis of one-hot encoded feature vectors used in training diagnostic classifiers.

---

### ⭐ Question B2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Set Operations and De Morgan's Application

**Q:** In a hospital database, define:
- $U = $ all 1000 patients
- $A = $ patients with **Fever** (320 patients)
- $B = $ patients with **Cough** (450 patients)
- $A \cap B = $ patients with **both** Fever and Cough (180 patients)

**Calculate:**
**(i)** $\vert A \cup B \vert$
**(ii)** $\vert A - B \vert$, $\vert B - A \vert$
**(iii)** $\vert (A \cup B)^c \vert$, $\vert A^c \cap B^c \vert$, and verify De Morgan's Law.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Union Cardinality (Inclusion-Exclusion):**

$$\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert = 320 + 450 - 180 = \boxed{590}$$

**(ii) Set Differences:**

$$\vert A - B \vert = \vert A \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert = 320 - 180 = 140$$

$$\vert B - A \vert = \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert = 450 - 180 = 270$$

**Sanity check:** $\vert A - B \vert + \vert A \cap B \vert + \vert B - A \vert = 140 + 180 + 270 = 590$ ✓

**(iii) De Morgan's Law Verification:**

$$\vert (A \cup B)^c \vert = \vert U \vert - \vert A \cup B \vert = 1000 - 590 = 410$$

$$\vert A^c \cap B^c \vert = \vert U \vert - \vert A \cup B \vert = 410 \quad \text{(by De Morgan's Law)}$$

**Direct verification via $A^c \cap B^c$:**

$$\vert A^c \vert = 1000 - 320 = 680$$
$$\vert B^c \vert = 1000 - 450 = 550$$
$$\vert A^c \cap B^c \vert = \vert A^c \vert + \vert B^c \vert - \vert A^c \cup B^c \vert$$

Since $A^c \cup B^c = (A \cap B)^c$ (De Morgan's dual), $\vert A^c \cup B^c \vert = 1000 - 180 = 820$.

$$\vert A^c \cap B^c \vert = 680 + 550 - 820 = 410 \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Both methods yield 410**, confirming De Morgan's Law: $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Venn Diagram Construction and Region Identification

**Q:** Draw a Venn diagram for two sets $A$ and $B$ inside a universal set $U$ of 100 elements, given that $\vert A \vert = 50$, $\vert B \vert = 40$, and $\vert A \cap B \vert = 15$. Label every region and explain each region in the context of a *disease screening program*.

**Model Solution:**

**Venn Diagram (textual representation with regions):**

```
            ┌────────── UNIVERSAL SET U (100) ──────────┐
            │                                            │
            │    ┌──── A (50) ────┐    ┌── B (40) ──┐    │
            │    │                │    │            │    │
            │    │   A - B = 35   │ AB │ B - A = 25 │    │
            │    │   (Fever only) │ 15 │ (Cough only)    │
            │    │                │    │            │    │
            │    └────────────────┘    └────────────┘    │
            │                                            │
            │        (A ∪ B)^c = 40 (No symptoms)       │
            └────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Region-by-Region Interpretation in a Disease Screening Program:**

| Region | Cardinality | Clinical Meaning |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $A \cap B$ | **15** | Patients with **both Fever and Cough** → Suspected co-infection, prioritize for differential testing |
| $A - B$ | **35** | Patients with **Fever only** → Possible viral infection; isolate and monitor |
| $B - A$ | **25** | Patients with **Cough only** → Possible respiratory allergy or early-stage infection |
| $(A \cup B)^c$ | **40** | Patients with **no symptoms** → Healthy cohort; serve as control group |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $35 + 15 + 25 + 40 = 115$... ❌ Wait, this doesn't sum to 100. **Correction:** The values must be recomputed properly: $\vert A \cap B \vert = 15$, $\vert A - B \vert = 50 - 15 = 35$, $\vert B - A \vert = 40 - 15 = 25$, $\vert (A \cup B)^c \vert = 100 - 65 = 35$. Then $35 + 15 + 25 + 35 = 110$... still off. **Recheck:** $\vert A \cup B \vert = 50 + 40 - 15 = 75$, so $\vert (A \cup B)^c \vert = 100 - 75 = 25$. Final: $35 + 15 + 25 + 25 = 100$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the intersection in Inclusion-Exclusion.** Students often write $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert$, leading to over-counting. **Always subtract $\vert A \cap B \vert$.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $\subseteq$ with $\subset$.** A *subset* ($\subseteq$) allows equality; a *proper subset* ($\subset$) does NOT. The empty set $\emptyset$ is a subset of EVERY set, but never a proper subset of $\emptyset$ itself.
>
> 3. **Reversing De Morgan's Laws.** The correct forms are:
>    - $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$ (complement of union = intersection of complements)
>    - $(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$ (complement of intersection = union of complements)
>    Many students swap them — **deduct 1 mark** for each such error.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the +1 in power set count.** $\vert \mathcal{P}(A) \vert = 2^n$, where $n$ is the cardinality. The result includes the empty set as one of the subsets.
>
> 5. **Treating Cartesian product as commutative.** $(a, b) \neq (b, a)$ in general, so $\vert A \times B \vert = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert$, NOT $\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert$.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the rectangle (Universal Set) in Venn diagrams.** KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing $U$.
>
> 7. **Not specifying the Universal Set before taking complements.** Without $U$, the complement is undefined. Always state $U$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Every KTU Exam!**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- [ ] **Set:** Well-defined, unordered collection of *distinct* elements.
- [ ] **Empty set $\emptyset$:** The set with no elements; $\vert \emptyset \vert = 0$.
- [ ] **Universal set $U$:** Contains all elements under consideration.
- [ ] **Subset $A \subseteq B$:** Every element of $A$ is in $B$.
- [ ] **Proper subset $A \subset B$:** $A \subseteq B$ AND $A \neq B$.
- [ ] **Power set $\mathcal{P}(A)$:** The set of *all* subsets of $A$.
- [ ] **Cardinality $\vert A \vert$:** Number of elements in $A$.

### 📌 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [ ] $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$
- [ ] $\vert \mathcal{P}(A) \vert = 2^{\vert A \vert}$
- [ ] $\vert A \times B \vert = \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert$
- [ ] $\vert A^c \vert = \vert U \vert - \vert A \vert$

### 📌 De Morgan's Laws (Laws of Negation)
- [ ] $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$
- [ ] $(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$

### 📌 Three-Set Inclusion-Exclusion (High-Weightage)
- [ ] $\vert A \cup B \cup C \vert = \sum \vert A_i \vert - \sum \vert A_i \cap A_j \vert + \vert A \cap B \cap C \vert$

### 📌 Symbol Recall
- [ ] $\in$ (element of), $\notin$ (not element of)
- [ ] $\subseteq$ (subset), $\subset$ (proper subset)
- [ ] $\cup$ (union), $\cap$ (intersection)
- [ ] $^c$ or $'$ (complement)
- [ ] $\triangle$ (symmetric difference)
- [ ] $\emptyset$ (empty set), $\mathcal{P}$ (power set)
- [ ] $\times$ (Cartesian product)

### 📌 Venn Diagram Mastery
- [ ] Two-set diagram: **4 regions** (A only, B only, A∩B, neither)
- [ ] Three-set diagram: **8 regions** (7 inside circles + 1 outside)
- [ ] Always include the rectangle representing $U$.

### 📌 Medical Informatics Hooks (Impress the Examiner)
- [ ] EHR queries are set operations on patient cohorts.
- [ ] ICD-10 disease codes define a universal set of diagnoses.
- [ ] Drug-drug interaction checkers use set intersection.
- [ ] Fuzzy sets model *partial* diagnostic membership (e.g., 70% likely positive).
- [ ] The Cartesian product underpins the Relational Database Model.

### 📌 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do not use the **same letter** for both a set and an element of that set.
- [ ] Do not confuse "subset" with "subsequence" (a set concept, not a sequence).
- [ ] Do not assume sets are ordered — $\{1, 2\} = \{2, 1\}$.
- [ ] Do not write $\{1, 1, 2\}$ — the duplicate $1$ is silently removed in set notation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** Remember the mnemonic **"UNION-OR, INTERSECTION-AND"** — and De Morgan's Laws by the rhyme *"break the line, swap the sign"* (break the union, swap to intersection, and complement each term).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Python Libraries - Pandas and Scikit-learn - Linear Regression Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT866 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Python Libraries - Pandas and Scikit-learn - Linear Regression Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pandas — The Data Manipulation Backbone

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Pandas** is an open-source, BSD-licensed Python library providing high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis tools. It is built on top of **NumPy** and is the de-facto standard for tabular, heterogeneous data manipulation in Python. Its two primary data structures are the one-dimensional **`Series`** and the two-dimensional **`DataFrame`**.

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠
Imagine a hospital's **OPD register book**. Each page is a table — columns are *Patient ID, Name, Age, BP, Sugar Level, Diagnosis*; each row is a unique patient. A Pandas **DataFrame** is exactly that digital register: you can sort by age, filter only diabetic patients, compute the average BP of a ward, or merge two registers (e.g., lab + pharmacy). Without Pandas, you would be reading CSV line-by-line with raw Python loops — slow, error-prone, and unscientific for medical data.

### Why Pandas in Medical Informatics?
- Handles **missing clinical values** (e.g., unrecorded HbA1c) gracefully.
- Built-in statistical functions: `mean()`, `std()`, `describe()`.
- Reads **HL7, CSV, Excel, SQL** tables — common medical data formats.
- Vectorized operations: **O(n)** speed on millions of EHR (Electronic Health Record) rows.

### Core Data Structures

| Structure | Dimensionality | Mutable | Heterogeneous | Use Case in Medicine |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Series` | 1-D | Yes | No (single dtype) | Tracking one patient's vitals over time |
| `DataFrame` | 2-D | Yes | Yes (column-wise) | Full patient cohort table |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A DataFrame's size is *mutable* (you can add/remove columns and rows) but its *value entries* are *immutable* in a single cell (you must use `.loc[]` or `.iloc[]` to modify). Remember this distinction for viva questions.

---

## 1.2 Scikit-learn (sklearn) — The Machine Learning Workhorse

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Scikit-learn** is a free, open-source Python library built on NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib that provides simple and efficient tools for **predictive data analysis** and **statistical modeling**, including classification, regression, clustering, dimensionality reduction, and model selection. It follows a uniform **`fit()` → `predict()` → `score()`** API.

### Conceptual Analogy 🎯
Think of Scikit-learn as a **well-stocked clinical laboratory**. You don't build the spectrophotometer from scratch; you walk in, hand over a blood sample (`fit()` with features + labels), the machine calibrates itself, and then gives you results for new samples (`predict()`). Scikit-learn does the same for ML: you give it data, it "calibrates" a model, and you query it.

### Why Scikit-learn in Medical Research?
- **Standardized API** — same `fit`/`predict` calls for 50+ algorithms.
- Built-in **train-test split**, **cross-validation**, **hyperparameter tuning**.
- Seamless integration with Pandas DataFrames.
- Used in published clinical prediction models (e.g., cardiovascular risk, sepsis onset).

---

## 1.3 Linear Regression Model — The Foundational Predictive Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Module-3)**
> **Linear Regression** is a **supervised machine learning** algorithm that models the relationship between a **scalar dependent variable (target, y)** and one or more **independent variables (features, X)** by fitting a linear equation to observed data. The simplest form, **Simple Linear Regression**, uses a single feature:
> $$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$$
> where $\beta_0$ is the **intercept** and $\beta_1$ is the **slope coefficient**.

### Conceptual Analogy 🩺
Imagine plotting a patient's **Body Mass Index (BMI)** (x-axis) against their **fasting blood glucose** (y-axis). Most points will fall in a clear upward-broadening cloud — heavier patients tend to have higher sugar. Linear regression draws the **single best straight line** through this cloud. That line is now a clinical screening tool: give it a new BMI, and it *predicts* the expected glucose. The "best" line is the one whose predicted values are, on average, closest to the actual measured values.

### Types of Linear Regression (KTU Syllabus)

| Type | Equation Form | Medical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Linear Regression** | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Predicting BP from age alone |
| **Multiple Linear Regression** | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n$ | Predicting diabetes risk from BMI + age + family history |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The word "linear" refers to linearity in the *coefficients* $\beta_i$, not necessarily in the features.** So $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$ is still called a *linear* regression model because it is linear in $\beta$. This is a frequently-asked KTU viva trap question.

---

## 1.4 Why These Three Concepts Belong Together in Medical Informatics

The medical data science workflow is a tight pipeline:
1. **Pandas** → Load, clean, and shape the EHR dataset (handle missing values, encode categorical variables, scale features).
2. **Scikit-learn** → Split data into train/test sets, instantiate the Linear Regression estimator, fit, predict, and evaluate.
3. **Linear Regression** → The actual mathematical model that converts patient features into a continuous clinical prediction (e.g., estimated GFR, hospital length-of-stay, drug dosage).

> [!TIP]
> A clean mental model: **Pandas = the kitchen** (prepares ingredients), **Linear Regression = the recipe** (the math), **Scikit-learn = the chef** (executes the recipe reliably).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear Regression Hypothesis

For a dataset with $m$ training examples and $n$ features, the hypothesis (prediction) for the $i$-th patient is:

$$\hat{y}^{(i)} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \beta_2 x_2^{(i)} + \dots + \beta_n x_n^{(i)} = \sum_{j=0}^{n} \beta_j x_j^{(i)}$$

where we define $x_0^{(i)} = 1$ for every $i$ (the bias trick), so $\beta_0$ becomes the intercept.

### In Vectorized (Matrix) Form

Let $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ be the feature row (with $x_0=1$ prepended) and $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ be the parameter vector. Then:

$$\hat{y}^{(i)} = \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

For the **entire dataset** with design matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta}$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ is the column vector of all predictions.

---

## 2.2 The Cost Function — Mean Squared Error (MSE)

The model is "good" when its predictions are close to the true labels. The standard measure is the **Mean Squared Error**:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

In matrix form:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y} \right)^\top \left( \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y} \right)$$

**Why the factor $\frac{1}{2}$?** It cancels the 2 produced when differentiating $x^2$ — purely a mathematical convenience. It does not change the location of the minimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MSE and not MAE (Mean Absolute Error)?**
> MSE is **differentiable everywhere** (MAE is not differentiable at zero), which is required by gradient-based optimizers like gradient descent. Also, MSE penalizes large clinical errors quadratically — a 10-unit glucose prediction error is penalized **100×** more than a 1-unit error, which is desirable when large medical errors are catastrophic.

---

## 2.3 Two Methods to Find the Optimal $\boldsymbol{\beta}$

### Method 1 — The Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution)

Set the gradient $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{0}$ and solve:

$$\boldsymbol{\beta}^* = \left( \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

### Method 2 — Gradient Descent (Iterative Optimization)

Update rule applied **simultaneously** for all $j$:

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial \beta_j}$$

where the partial derivative is:

$$\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

and $\alpha > 0$ is the **learning rate**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the single most important reference for KTU 2024 exam answers. Memorize the formula, the units, and one medical example for each.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Units | Medical Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple LR model | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Line equation, dimensionless ratio if both scaled | Glucose (mg/dL) vs BMI (kg/m²) |
| 2 | Multiple LR (vector) | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ | $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ | Predicting systolic BP from age, BMI, sodium intake |
| 3 | MSE Cost | $J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$ | Unit: (target unit)², e.g., (mg/dL)² | Average squared glucose error |
| 4 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2}$ | Same unit as $y$ | Clinical-grade error magnitude |
| 5 | MAE | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{m}\sum \vert \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \vert$ | Same unit as $y$ | Robust to outliers |
| 6 | $R^2$ (Coefficient of Determination) | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}{\sum(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le R^2 \le 1$ | Variance explained by the model |
| 7 | Normal Equation | $\boldsymbol{\beta}^* = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form, $O(n^3)$ | Best for $n \le 10^4$ features |
| 8 | Gradient Descent Update | $\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ for medical data | Best for large $n$ or streaming data |
| 9 | Multicollinearity Check | VIF $= \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2}$ | VIF > 5 ⇒ drop feature | Avoid redundant lab tests as inputs |
| 10 | Confidence Interval for $\beta_j$ | $\beta_j \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, m-n-1} \cdot \text{SE}(\beta_j)$ | 95 % CI typical | Statistical significance of a biomarker |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall:** In Markdown tables, never write `|x|`. Use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\lvert x \rvert$`. The KTU answer-script grader will not reformat, so broken tables will cost you.

---

## 2.5 Evaluation Metrics — Deep Dive

### $R^2$ (R-squared)
$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{res}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}}$$

- $\text{SS}_{\text{res}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ — residual sum of squares.
- $\text{SS}_{\text{tot}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})^2$ — total sum of squares.
- **Interpretation:** The fraction of variance in $y$ that the model explains.
- $R^2 = 1$: perfect prediction; $R^2 = 0$: model is no better than predicting the mean.

### Adjusted $R^2$ (penalizes extra features)
$$R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \left(1 - R^2\right) \frac{m - 1}{m - n - 1}$$

Use this when comparing models with different numbers of features — vital in clinical research where every added lab test costs money.

---

## 2.6 Assumptions of Linear Regression (KTU Favourite Viva Topic)

1. **Linearity** — Relationship between $X$ and $y$ is linear in coefficients.
2. **Independence** of observations (no autocorrelation; important in longitudinal patient data).
3. **Homoscedasticity** — Constant variance of residuals across all predicted values.
4. **Normality of residuals** — Errors are normally distributed (matters for p-values, less so for prediction).
5. **No multicollinearity** — Features are not highly correlated with each other (check via VIF).
6. **No measurement error in $X$** — Regressors are measured exactly (often violated in clinical datasets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Linear regression is the backbone of **clinical decision-support systems** (e.g., the MDRD and CKD-EPI equations for estimating Glomerular Filtration Rate from serum creatinine, age, sex, and race), **pharmacokinetic dose-response modeling**, and **hospital resource forecasting**.

---

## 2.7 Why Gradient Descent vs. Normal Equation? (Comparative Analysis)

| Criterion | Normal Equation | Gradient Descent |
|---|---|---|
| Need to choose $\alpha$? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Need feature scaling? | **No** | **Yes** (helps convergence) |
| Number of iterations? | **None** (1-shot) | **Many** (could be 10⁶) |
| Time complexity | $O(n^3)$ — invert $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ | $O(knm)$ — $k$ iterations |
| Works when $n > m$? | **Slow / singular** | **Yes** |
| Works for non-linear models? | **No** | **Yes** (with appropriate gradient) |

**Rule of thumb used in medical data science:** Use Normal Equation for $n \le 10,000$ and Gradient Descent (or its variants Adam, L-BFGS in scikit-learn) for larger feature sets or when $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is ill-conditioned.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Equation (Exhaustive)

We start from the MSE cost function in matrix form:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y} \right)^\top \left( \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y} \right)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the quadratic term.** Using the identity $(A - B)^\top (A - B) = A^\top A - 2A^\top B + B^\top B$ with $A = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and $B = \mathbf{y}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) &= \frac{1}{2m} \Big[ (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) - 2(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y} \Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{2m} \Big[ \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y} \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic note:** $(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top = \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top$ because $(AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top$. This step is what allows us to write everything in terms of the column vector $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.** Using the matrix calculus rules:
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} (\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\beta}) = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ (when $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric, and $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is symmetric).
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} (\mathbf{b}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{b}$.
- $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} (\text{constant}) = \mathbf{0}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) &= \frac{1}{2m} \Big[ 2 \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - 2 \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{m} \Big[ \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Set gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition).**

$$\frac{1}{m} \Big[ \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \Big] = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.** Drop the scalar $\frac{1}{m}$ (it does not affect the zero):

$$\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.** Provided $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ is invertible (i.e., features are linearly independent and $m \ge n$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} &= \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \\
(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}) \boldsymbol{\beta} &= (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \\
\mathbf{I} \cdot \boldsymbol{\beta} &= (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\beta}^* = \left( \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}}$$

This is the **Normal Equation** — the closed-form, optimal parameter vector that globally minimizes the MSE.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Toy Clinical Dataset)

Consider a synthetic dataset of $m = 5$ patients with a single feature (daily steps in thousands) and target (resting heart rate in bpm). The bias column $x_0 = 1$ is prepended.

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 6 \\ 1 & 8 \\ 1 & 10 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 85 \\ 78 \\ 72 \\ 70 \\ 65 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 6 \\ 1 & 8 \\ 1 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 30 \\ 30 & 220 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 85 \\ 78 \\ 72 \\ 70 \\ 65 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 85 + 78 + 72 + 70 + 65 \\ 170 + 312 + 432 + 560 + 650 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 370 \\ 2124 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inverse of $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$.** For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & d \end{bmatrix}$ with determinant $\Delta = ad - b^2$, the inverse is $\frac{1}{\Delta}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -b & a \end{bmatrix}$.

$$\Delta = (5)(220) - (30)(30) = 1100 - 900 = 200$$

$$(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{200}\begin{bmatrix} 220 & -30 \\ -30 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.10 & -0.15 \\ -0.15 & 0.025 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\boldsymbol{\beta}^*$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\beta}^* &= (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1.10 & -0.15 \\ -0.15 & 0.025 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 370 \\ 2124 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (1.10)(370) + (-0.15)(2124) \\ (-0.15)(370) + (0.025)(2124) \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 407.0 - 318.6 \\ -55.5 + 53.1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 88.4 \\ -2.4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the coefficients.**
- $\beta_0 = 88.4$ — Predicted HR (bpm) when daily steps = 0 (a sedentary patient).
- $\beta_1 = -2.4$ — Each additional 1000 daily steps **decreases** HR by 2.4 bpm. Clinically meaningful and negative (as expected).

The fitted regression line is:

$$\hat{y} = 88.4 - 2.4 \cdot x$$

**Step 6 — Verify by hand for the first patient ($x = 2$):**

$$\hat{y}^{(1)} = 88.4 - 2.4(2) = 88.4 - 4.8 = 83.6 \text{ bpm} \quad (\text{actual} = 85)$$

The residual $e^{(1)} = 85 - 83.6 = 1.4$ bpm. Small, so the fit is good.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule

We re-derive the partial derivative of $J(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ w.r.t. a single $\beta_j$ from first principles.

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \dots + \beta_n x_n^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

Applying the chain rule, with $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \dots + \beta_n x_n^{(i)}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} &= \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial \beta_j} \\
&= \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)} \\
&= \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Gradient Descent Update:**

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Algorithm (Batch Gradient Descent):**
1. Initialize $\beta_j = 0$ for all $j$ (or small random values).
2. **Repeat until convergence** (e.g., $J(\boldsymbol{\beta})$ changes by less than $10^{-6}$):
   a. Compute $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$.
   b. Compute the error vector $\mathbf{e} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$.
   c. For each $j$: update $\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i} e^{(i)} x_j^{(i)}$.
3. Return $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.

> [!TIP]
> In **scikit-learn**, the `LinearRegression` class internally uses the **Normal Equation** (via `np.linalg.lstsq` for numerical stability) rather than gradient descent. The `SGDRegressor` class uses stochastic gradient descent if you need the iterative approach.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table — Pandas vs. Raw Python (Why Pandas Wins)

| Task | Raw Python (list-of-lists) | Pandas DataFrame |
|---|---|---|
| Load 100k patient records | Manual CSV parsing, ~30 lines | `pd.read_csv('ehr.csv')` (1 line) |
| Compute mean BP of ward | Loop accumulator | `df['BP'].mean()` (1 line) |
| Filter diabetic patients | Nested if-loops | `df[df['Diagnosis'] == 'Diabetes']` (1 line) |
| Handle missing HbA1c | Custom `None` checks | `df['HbA1c'].fillna(df['HbA1c'].median())` (1 line) |
| Group by hospital | Custom dict-of-lists | `df.groupby('Hospital').agg({'BP':'mean'})` (1 line) |
| Merge lab + pharmacy | Nested loops | `pd.merge(lab_df, pharma_df, on='PatientID')` (1 line) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pandas operations are vectorized** — internally compiled in C, so a 1M-row DataFrame operation is 100–1000× faster than a Python loop. In medical informatics where datasets routinely exceed 10⁵ rows, this is non-negotiable.

---

## 3.5 Feature Scaling — A Prerequisite for Gradient Descent

If features have very different units (e.g., Age in years [0–100] vs. Sodium in mEq/L [135–145] vs. Income in ₹ [10⁴–10⁶]), the cost function $J$ becomes an elongated ellipsoid in $\boldsymbol{\beta}$-space, and gradient descent zig-zags.

**Standardization (Z-score scaling):**

$$x_j^{\text{scaled}} = \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$

This makes every feature have mean 0 and standard deviation 1, transforming the cost function into a near-spherical bowl — gradient descent converges in a straight line.

**Note:** The Normal Equation **does not** require feature scaling, only gradient descent does.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End Linear Regression Pipeline in Medical Informatics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Clinical Study] --> loadB[Load EHR Dataset using Pandas read_csv]
    loadB --> inspectC[Inspect with df.head, df.info, df.describe]
    inspectC --> cleanD[Data Cleaning: handle missing values, remove duplicates, fix dtype]
    cleanD --> encodeE[Encode Categorical Variables: one hot encoding or label encoding]
    encodeE --> splitF[Train Test Split using sklearn train_test_split, 80 20]
    splitF --> scaleG[Optional Feature Scaling using StandardScaler]
    scaleG --> fitH[Instantiate LinearRegression and call fit on X train, y train]
    fitH --> coefI[Extract beta coefficients and intercept from model]
    fitI[Fit Computed] --> predJ[Call predict on X test to get y hat]
    predJ --> evalK[Evaluate using MSE, MAE, RMSE, R squared]
    evalK --> plotL[Diagnostic Plots: residuals vs fitted, Q Q plot, leverage]
    plotL --> validM[Clinical Validation: consult domain expert, sign off]
    validM --> deployN[Deploy as Clinical Decision Support API]
    deployN --> endO[End: Model in production]

    style startA fill:#90EE90
    style endO fill:#FFB6C1
    style fitH fill:#87CEEB
    style evalK fill:#FFD700
    style deployN fill:#DDA0DD
```

> **Reading guide:** Green nodes = process start, Blue = core ML step, Gold = evaluation, Pink = deployment, Lavender = end. Note the strict linearity: every block depends on the previous one. Skipping the `cleanD` or `encodeE` step is the #1 reason for poor clinical model performance in practice.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Pandas, Scikit-learn, and NumPy Interplay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    csvA[CSV / Excel / SQL Medical Records] --> pandasB[Pandas DataFrame]
    pandasB --> numpyC[NumPy Arrays via df.values or to_numpy]
    numpyC --> sklearnD[Scikit learn Estimator e.g. LinearRegression]
    sklearnD --> numpyE[NumPy Predictions and Coefficients]
    numpyE --> pandasF[Predictions Series joined back to DataFrame]
    pandasF --> reportG[Clinical Report CSV or PDF]
    sklearnD --> metricsH[sklearn metrics module MSE MAE R2]
    metricsH --> reportG
    pandasB --> plotI[Matplotlib / Seaborn Visualizations]

    style csvA fill:#FFFACD
    style pandasB fill:#90EE90
    style numpyC fill:#ADD8E6
    style sklearnD fill:#FFB6C1
    style reportG fill:#DDA0DD
```

> **Reading guide:** Pandas is the *outer shell* (data wrangling), NumPy is the *inner engine* (vectorized math), Scikit-learn sits on top of NumPy and accepts DataFrames or NumPy arrays transparently. Visualizations (Matplotlib/Seaborn) are diagnostic tools that consume DataFrames directly.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Gradient Descent Convergence Behavior

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitializeBeta
    InitializeBeta --> ComputePredictions: beta initial 0
    ComputePredictions --> ComputeCost: y hat equals X times beta
    ComputeCost --> CheckConvergence: J beta equals MSE
    CheckConvergence --> Converged: change in J less than 1e to the 6
    CheckConvergence --> ComputeGradients: not converged
    ComputeGradients --> UpdateBeta: gradient equals 1 over m times X transpose times X beta minus y
    UpdateBeta --> ComputePredictions: beta equals beta minus alpha times gradient
    Converged --> ExtractCoefficients
    ExtractCoefficients --> [*]
```

> **Reading guide:** The loop `ComputePredictions → ComputeCost → CheckConvergence → ComputeGradients → UpdateBeta` repeats hundreds to thousands of times. The `Converged` exit condition is essential — without it, the algorithm would never terminate.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Conceptual Diagram — Geometric Intuition of Linear Regression

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dataA[Patient Data Points in 2D: BMI on x axis, Glucose on y axis] --> lineB[Fitted Regression Line y hat equals beta0 plus beta1 x]
    lineB --> residC[Vertical Residuals: y minus y hat, the gaps]
    residC --> mink[D[Minimize Sum of Squared Residuals: Ordinary Least Squares OLS]]
    lineB --> predictE[New Patient with BMI equals 32: predict Glucose on line]
    predictE --> clinicF[Clinical Decision: screen for diabetes if predicted Glucose greater than 126 mg per dL]

    style dataA fill:#FFB6C1
    style lineB fill:#90EE90
    style mink fill:#FFD700
    style clinicF fill:#87CEEB
```

> **Reading guide:** The pink nodes are the *input* and the *clinical output*; the green node is the *learned model*; the gold node is the *optimization objective*. This is the single most important mental picture for the KTU viva.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Bias-Variance Trade-off (Position of Linear Regression)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    underA[Underfitting: High Bias, Low Variance, model too simple] --> linB[Linear Regression sweet spot for linearly separable clinical data]
    overC[Overfitting: Low Bias, High Variance, model too complex, e.g. deep polynomial]
    linB --> regD[Regularized Linear Regression Ridge LASSO Elastic Net as upgrade path]
    regD --> overC

    style underA fill:#FF6347
    style linB fill:#90EE90
    style overC fill:#FF6347
    style regD fill:#FFD700
```

> **Reading guide:** Simple linear regression sits in the *sweet spot* (green) for many medical problems with $n \le 10$ features and $m \ge 30n$ samples. For higher complexity, regularization (Ridge / LASSO) is the next step before going to non-linear models.

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Linear Regression model. Differentiate between Simple and Multiple Linear Regression with one medical example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Linear Regression is a supervised machine learning algorithm that models the linear relationship between a continuous target variable $y$ and one or more predictor variables $X$ by fitting the equation $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n$, where $\beta_0$ is the intercept and $\beta_1, \dots, \beta_n$ are the slope coefficients estimated by minimizing the Mean Squared Error (MSE).

> **Difference Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Linear Regression | Multiple Linear Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Number of predictors | One ($x$) | Two or more ($x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$) |
| Equation | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots$ |
| Medical example | Predicting heart rate from age | Predicting heart rate from age, BMI, and cholesterol |
| Geometric form | Straight line in 2-D | Hyperplane in $(n+1)$-D |

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. The two alternatives below are independent — answer EITHER (Q.A) OR (Q.B). Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Pandas + Linear Regression Theory

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Set A]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a Pandas `DataFrame`. With a neat labeled diagram, describe the difference between `loc[]` and `iloc[]` accessors. Write a Python snippet (you may write the code in description form, not execute) to: (i) create a DataFrame of 5 patients with columns *PatientID, Age, BP, Glucose*; (ii) display only the rows where *Glucose > 140*; (iii) compute the mean BP of those patients.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Normal Equation for a Multiple Linear Regression model with $n$ predictors and $m$ training examples, starting from the MSE cost function in matrix form. State two conditions under which the Normal Equation fails and one alternative in each case.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**DataFrame Structure (2 marks):**
A `DataFrame` is a **2-D, labeled, size-mutable, heterogeneous tabular** data structure with three components:
- **Data** — stored as a 2-D NumPy array (values).
- **Row index** (axis 0) — labels for rows, e.g., patient IDs.
- **Column index** (axis 1) — labels for columns, e.g., Age, BP, Glucose.

**`loc[]` vs `iloc[]` (2 marks):**

| Accessor | Indexing Basis | Example | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| `df.loc[]` | **Label-based** (row/column names) | `df.loc[2, 'BP']` | Value at row label 2, column 'BP' |
| `df.iloc[]` | **Integer-position-based** (0-indexed) | `df.iloc[2, 1]` | Value at 3rd row, 2nd column |

**Conceptual diagram (drawn in the answer book):**
```
        Columns →  'PatientID'  'Age'  'BP'  'Glucose'
Row idx ↓
0                  101          45     130   145
1                  102          60     145   180
2                  103          34     118   95
3                  104          55     140   165
4                  105          29     122   88
```
- `df.loc[2, 'BP']` → `118`
- `df.iloc[2, 2]` → `118` (same cell, different access style)

**Pseudocode/Description for the operations (3 marks):**
1. **Create DataFrame** (1 mark): Use `pd.DataFrame({ 'PatientID': [101, 102, 103, 104, 105], 'Age': [45, 60, 34, 55, 29], 'BP': [130, 145, 118, 140, 122], 'Glucose': [145, 180, 95, 165, 88] })`.
2. **Filter rows** (1 mark): `df[df['Glucose'] > 140]` — this Boolean indexing returns only the 3 patients (101, 102, 104) with Glucose above 140.
3. **Mean BP** (1 mark): `df[df['Glucose'] > 140]['BP'].mean()` — computes the mean BP of the filtered subset. For our data: $\frac{130 + 145 + 140}{3} = 138.33$ mmHg.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The concise 7-mark version expected in the exam is:

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the MSE in matrix form:
> $$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m}(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y})^\top(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y})$$

> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Expand using $(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top = \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top$:
> $$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m}[\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y}]$$

> **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ using matrix calculus rules:
> $$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J = \frac{1}{m}[\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}]$$

> **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Set the gradient to zero and solve:
> $$\boldsymbol{\beta}^* = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

> **Step 5 (2 marks) — Failure conditions & alternatives:**

| # | Failure Condition | Alternative Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ is **non-invertible** (singular) when features are linearly dependent (e.g., height in cm and height in inches) or $m < n$ | **Gradient Descent** or **Ridge Regression** (adds $\lambda I$ to make matrix invertible) |
| 2 | $n$ is very large ($\ge 10^5$), making the $O(n^3)$ inversion computationally infeasible | **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** or **Mini-Batch GD** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** skip showing the matrix expansion step (1 mark reserved for it).
> - Do **not** write only the final formula — that is **0 marks** for derivation.
> - When stating alternatives, write the **method name AND the reason it works** (e.g., "Ridge adds $\lambda I$ for invertibility"). Half-credit only for naming without reason.
