
> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram is the **medical-informatics-centric view** of how raw genome data flows from a wet-lab sample to a clinically actionable report — a frequent KTU Part B question topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Gene (ii) Genome (iii) Genomics**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Gene:** A gene is the basic physical and functional unit of heredity, consisting of a specific DNA sequence that codes for a functional product (protein/RNA). **Example:** The *BRCA1* gene on chromosome 17 codes for a tumor-suppressor protein linked to breast cancer.
- **(ii) Genome:** The complete set of DNA (including all genes and non-coding sequences) present in an organism. **Example:** The human genome contains approximately 3.2 billion base pairs across 46 chromosomes.
- **(iii) Genomics:** The interdisciplinary field of science that studies the structure, function, evolution, and mapping of genomes using high-throughput sequencing and bioinformatics. **Example:** The Human Genome Project (HGP) is a landmark genomics initiative completed in 2003.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of the Central Dogma of molecular biology. Mention any two exceptions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Central Dogma**, proposed by Francis Crick (1958), describes the unidirectional flow of genetic information in a cell:

$$\text{DNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Replication}} \text{DNA} \rightarrow \text{mRNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Translation}} \text{Protein}$$

**Two Exceptions:**
1. **Reverse Transcription** — In retroviruses (e.g., HIV), the enzyme **reverse transcriptase** synthesizes DNA from an RNA template: $\text{RNA} \rightarrow \text{DNA}$.
2. **RNA Replication** — In some RNA viruses (e.g., poliovirus, influenza), RNA-dependent RNA polymerase copies RNA directly: $\text{RNA} \rightarrow \text{RNA}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, describe the double-helix structure of DNA as proposed by Watson and Crick. Highlight the base-pairing rule and the antiparallel nature of the strands.

**Model Answer:**

The Watson-Crick model of DNA (1953) describes DNA as a **right-handed double helix** with the following features:

**Structural Features:**
- DNA consists of **two polynucleotide strands** wound around a common axis.
- Each strand has a **sugar-phosphate backbone** on the outside and **nitrogenous bases** projecting inward.
- The helix completes **one full turn every 3.4 nm**, containing **10 base pairs** per turn.
- The diameter of the helix is **2 nm**.
- Strands run in **antiparallel** orientation: one strand is 5' → 3', the other is 3' → 5'.

**Base-Pairing Rule (Chargaff's Rule):**
- **Adenine (A)** pairs with **Thymine (T)** via **2 hydrogen bonds**.
- **Guanine (G)** pairs with **Cytosine (C)** via **3 hydrogen bonds**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A : T} &\rightleftharpoons \text{2 Hydrogen bonds} \\
\text{G} \equiv \text{C} &\rightleftharpoons \text{3 Hydrogen bonds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance of Antiparallel Orientation:**
- Allows **complementary base pairing** in opposite directions.
- Critical for **semiconservative DNA replication**, where each strand serves as a template for a new complementary strand.

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
   5' ---P--S--B--S--P--S--B--S--P--- 3'   (Strand 1)
        |        |        |
   3' ---P--S--B--S--P--S--B--S--P--- 5'   (Strand 2)
   (P = Phosphate, S = Sugar, B = Base)
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the process of **transcription** in eukaryotes with a flowchart. What is RNA splicing?

**Model Answer:**

**Transcription** is the synthesis of mRNA from a DNA template, occurring in the **nucleus** and catalysed by **RNA polymerase II**.

**Stages of Transcription:**

1. **Initiation:**
   - Transcription factors (TFIID, TFIIB, etc.) bind to the **promoter** (TATA box).
   - RNA polymerase II is recruited to form the **pre-initiation complex**.

2. **Elongation:**
   - RNA polymerase moves 3' → 5' along the **template (antisense) strand**.
   - Ribonucleotides are added 5' → 3' to form a complementary **pre-mRNA**.
   - The non-template (sense/coding) strand has the same sequence as mRNA (with U replacing T).

3. **Termination:**
   - RNA polymerase II transcribes a **polyadenylation signal (AAUAAA)**.
   - Pre-mRNA is cleaved and released.

**Post-Transcriptional Modifications:**
- **5' Capping:** A 7-methylguanosine cap is added to the 5' end (protects mRNA, aids ribosome binding).
- **3' Poly-A Tail:** ~200 adenine nucleotides added to the 3' end (enhances stability).
- **RNA Splicing:** Removal of **introns** (non-coding) and joining of **exons** (coding) by the **spliceosome** (a complex of snRNPs and proteins).

**Splicing Significance:**
- A single gene can produce **multiple protein variants** via **alternative splicing** — a key driver of human proteomic diversity.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the Human Genome Project? List its major goals and highlight five major findings.

**Model Answer:**

The **Human Genome Project (HGP)** was an international, collaborative research program formally launched in **October 1990** and completed in **April 2003**. Coordinated by the **U.S. Department of Energy (DOE)** and the **National Institutes of Health (NIH)**, it aimed to map and sequence the entire human genome.

**Major Goals:**
1. Identify all **~20,000 – 25,000 genes** in human DNA.
2. Determine the **complete sequence** of the 3 billion chemical base pairs.
3. Store this information in **public databases** (GenBank, Ensembl, UCSC Genome Browser).
4. Improve **data analysis tools** (BLAST, BLAT).
5. Address the **ethical, legal, and social issues (ELSI)** arising from genome data.

**Five Major Findings:**
1. The human genome contains **~3.2 billion base pairs**.
2. There are only about **20,000 – 25,000 protein-coding genes**, far fewer than the earlier estimate of ~100,000.
3. Only **~1.5%** of the genome codes for proteins; the rest includes regulatory regions, non-coding RNAs, introns, and historical transposable elements.
4. The **vast majority** of the genome is shared across humans (~99.9% identity); individual variation arises from **single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs)**.
5. Humans share a substantial number of genes with simple organisms (e.g., ~50% with **banana**, ~60% with **fruit fly**, ~98% with **chimpanzee**), supporting common evolutionary origin.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the salient features of the genetic code. Why is the code described as "degenerate" and "universal"?

**Model Answer:**

The **genetic code** is the set of rules by which information encoded in mRNA codons is translated into proteins by the ribosome.

**Salient Features:**

1. **Triplet Code:** Each codon consists of **3 nucleotides**, giving $4^3 = 64$ possible codons.
2. **Commaless and Non-overlapping:** Codons are read sequentially without gaps or overlap.
3. **Unambiguous:** One specific codon always codes for the same amino acid (e.g., AUG → Methionine).
4. **Degenerate (Redundant):** 61 codons code for 20 amino acids, so most amino acids are specified by more than one codon (e.g., Leucine by 6 codons).
5. **Universal:** The code is nearly identical in all organisms — from bacteria to humans — indicating evolutionary conservation.
6. **Start and Stop Signals:** **AUG** acts as the start codon (Methionine); **UAA, UAG, UGA** are stop codons.
7. **Polarity:** The code has a fixed reading direction (5' → 3').

**Why "Degenerate"?**
Because **multiple codons specify the same amino acid** (degeneracy), the code is said to be redundant. This protects against the harmful effects of point mutations — a mutation in the third position of a codon often does not change the amino acid (this is called the **wobble hypothesis**, proposed by Francis Crick, where the third base pairing is less stringent).

**Why "Universal"?**
The same codon specifies the same amino acid across virtually all species. For example, **UUU codes for Phenylalanine in humans, bacteria, plants, and viruses**. Minor exceptions exist in **mitochondria** and a few protozoans (e.g., UGA codes for Tryptophan in mitochondria, not a stop signal).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing the **template strand** with the **coding strand** — many students lose 2 marks by writing the mRNA sequence incorrectly.
> 2. Forgetting that **eukaryotic transcription occurs in the nucleus** but **translation occurs in the cytoplasm** (a 1-mark trap question).
> 3. Stating the number of human genes as **3.2 billion** — that is the number of **base pairs**, NOT the number of genes (which is ~20,000–25,000).
> 4. Missing the **wobble hypothesis** explanation in degeneracy questions.
> 5. Failing to mention **spliceosome** and **snRNPs** in RNA splicing answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Gene** = functional unit of heredity; **Genome** = complete DNA of an organism; **Genomics** = study of genomes.
- [x] DNA is a **right-handed double helix**, antiparallel, with **10 bp per turn** and **2 nm** diameter.
- [x] **Chargaff's Rule:** A = T (2 H-bonds), G ≡ C (3 H-bonds).
- [x] DNA packaging: Nucleotide → DNA → Nucleosome (histone octamer) → 30 nm fiber → Looped domain → Chromosome.
- [x] **Central Dogma:** DNA → RNA → Protein; exceptions include **reverse transcription** and **RNA replication**.
- [x] **Genetic Code:** 64 codons, 61 sense + 3 stop; **triplet, universal, degenerate, unambiguous, commaless**.
- [x] **Eukaryotic gene structure:** Promoter → 5' UTR → Exons/Introns → 3' UTR → Terminator.
- [x] **Transcription** in nucleus, **Translation** in cytoplasm; RNA polymerase II synthesizes mRNA.
- [x] **Splicing** removes introns via spliceosome; **alternative splicing** increases proteome diversity.
- [x] **Human Genome Project (HGP):** Launched 1990, completed April 2003; ~3.2 Gb, ~20,000–25,000 genes, only ~1.5% codes for proteins.
- [x] Tools of genomics informatics: **BLAST, BioPython, Galaxy, GATK, ClinVar, dbSNP, VCF format, HL7/FHIR integration**.
- [x] Real-world applications: **Personalized medicine, pharmacogenomics, GWAS, predictive diagnostics, CRISPR gene editing**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DNA Sequencing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧬 DNA Sequencing — Decoding the Blueprint of Life

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT866 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DNA Sequencing

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **DNA Sequencing** is the laboratory process of determining the precise order of nucleotides — **Adenine (A)**, **Thymine (T)**, **Cytosine (C)**, and **Guanine (G)** — within a DNA molecule. The technique enables the conversion of biological genetic information into a digital, character-based string (e.g., `ATGCGCTAG...`) that can be stored, analyzed, and compared computationally.

### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy: "Reading a Book Written in a 4-Letter Alphabet"

Imagine a very long book (the human genome has roughly **3.2 billion letters** spread across **23 chromosomes**). The book is written in just 4 letters: **A, T, C, G**, which combine into words called *codons* that spell out instructions to build proteins. DNA sequencing is like a **super-fast typist** that reads this book letter-by-letter, but unlike a normal book, sequencing machines read it in **millions of short fragments simultaneously** and a computer (the assembler) stitches those fragments back together — like solving a giant jigsaw puzzle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Key Terms to Master:**
> - **Nucleotide**: The monomeric building block of DNA (A, T, C, G)
> - **Read**: A short, sequenced DNA fragment
> - **Coverage / Depth**: How many times a given nucleotide is sequenced on average
> - **Read Length**: Number of contiguous bases produced in a single sequencing reaction
> - **Throughput**: Total bases generated per run (measured in **Megabases (Mb)** or **Gigabases (Gb)**)

### 🧬 Why DNA Sequencing Matters in Medical Informatics

| Medical Application | Role of Sequencing |
|---|---|
| **Disease Diagnosis** | Identifies pathogenic mutations (e.g., BRCA1/BRCA2 in breast cancer) |
| **Pharmacogenomics** | Predicts drug response based on patient genotype |
| **Personalized Medicine** | Tailors treatment to individual genetic profile |
| **Infectious Disease** | Tracks viral/bacterial strains (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 variants) |
| **Forensic Medicine** | DNA fingerprinting for identity confirmation |
| **Prenatal Screening** | Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing (NIPT) using cell-free DNA |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generational Evolution of DNA Sequencing

DNA sequencing technology has evolved through **four major generations**, each overcoming the limitations of its predecessor:

- **First Generation (1977–2000s):** Sanger Method & Maxam-Gilbert Method
  - Long reads (500–1000 bp), low throughput, expensive per base
  - Used to sequence the **first human genome (Human Genome Project, 2003)** costing ~**$2.7 billion**

- **Second Generation / Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) (2005–Present):** Illumina, Ion Torrent
  - Short reads (50–300 bp), massively parallel, high throughput (**hundreds of Gb per run**)
  - Cost dropped to under **$600 per whole genome (2024)**

- **Third Generation (2010–Present):** Pacific Biosciences (PacBio SMRT), Oxford Nanopore
  - Long reads (**>10,000 bp**, PacBio HiFi: **>99.9% accuracy**)
  - Single-molecule real-time sequencing

- **Fourth Generation:** Single-cell sequencing, in-situ sequencing
  - Reads genetic information **inside individual cells** without destroying them

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The exam frequently asks students to **compare Sanger vs. NGS** and explain *why* NGS dominates modern clinical diagnostics. Focus on **throughput, cost, read length, and error rate**.

---

### 2.2 Sanger Sequencing (Chain Termination Method) — The Gold Standard

Developed by **Frederick Sanger (1977)**, this method uses **dideoxynucleotides (ddNTPs)** that lack a 3'-OH group, causing DNA polymerase to terminate extension.

**Operational Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Denaturation:** Heat the double-stranded DNA template to ~**95°C** to separate the strands
- **Step 2 — Primer Annealing:** Cool to ~**55°C** so a short primer binds the template
- **Step 3 — Extension & Termination:** DNA polymerase adds normal **dNTPs**, but occasionally incorporates a fluorescently-labeled **ddNTP** (A, T, C, or G), each tagged with a **distinct color**
- **Step 4 — Separation:** Fragments are separated by **capillary gel electrophoresis** based on size
- **Step 5 — Detection:** A laser excites the fluorophore; the emitted color identifies the terminal base
- **Step 6 — Analysis:** Software reconstructs the sequence from the ordered fragment ladder

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works:** Each ddNTP lacks a 3'-hydroxyl group, so once incorporated, **no further phosphodiester bond can form** — the chain is permanently terminated at that position.

---

### 2.3 Maxam-Gilbert Sequencing (Chemical Degradation Method)

Developed by **Allan Maxam and Walter Gilbert (1977)**, this method uses **chemical reagents to cleave DNA at specific bases**:

| Base | Chemical Cleavage Reagent |
|---|---|
| **G** | Dimethyl sulfate (methylates G, then piperidine cleaves) |
| **A + G** | Formic acid (depurinates A and G) |
| **C + T** | Hydrazine (reacts with C and T) |
| **C** | Hydrazine + NaCl (selective for C only) |

The resulting fragments are separated by electrophoresis, and a parallel set of 4 lanes (G, A+G, C+T, C) reveals the sequence. Maxam-Gilbert is now mostly **obsolete in clinical practice** because it uses hazardous chemicals and is harder to scale.

---

### 2.4 Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) — Illumina Platform

The **Illumina sequencing-by-synthesis (SBS)** platform dominates ~**70% of global sequencing market**.

**Workflow Steps:**

- **Library Preparation:** DNA is fragmented (~**200–500 bp**), adapter sequences are ligated to both ends
- **Cluster Generation:** Fragments bind to a **flow cell** coated with complementary adapters; **bridge amplification** creates clusters of clonal DNA copies
- **Sequencing by Synthesis:** Modified DNA polymerase incorporates fluorescently-labeled reversible terminators; each base emits a unique color captured by a camera
- **Imaging & Base Calling:** Software converts color images into nucleotide sequences (FASTQ format)
- **Alignment & Assembly:** Reads are mapped to a reference genome using algorithms like **BWA (Burrows-Wheeler Aligner)** or **Bowtie2**

> [!NOTE]
> **Paired-End Sequencing:** Both ends of a DNA fragment are sequenced, producing two reads in opposite orientations. This **dramatically improves alignment accuracy** and helps resolve repetitive regions.

---

### 2.5 Third-Generation Sequencing

| Platform | Underlying Principle | Key Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **PacBio SMRT** | Single Molecule Real-Time sequencing; DNA polymerase immobilized in **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs)** | Very long reads (**>15 kb**), detects **epigenetic modifications** (methylation) |
| **Oxford Nanopore** | DNA passes through a **biological nanopore**; changes in electrical current identify each base | Ultra-long reads (**>1 Mb possible**), portable device (**MinION** fits in a USB stick) |

---

### 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phred Quality Score** | $Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$ | $P$ = probability that a base is called **incorrectly** | Dimensionless (integer) |
| **Accuracy from Q-score** | $\text{Accuracy} = (1 - 10^{-Q/10}) \times 100\%$ | E.g., Q20 = **99% accuracy**, Q30 = **99.9%** | Percentage |
| **Coverage Depth (Mean)** | $D = \frac{N \times L}{G}$ | $N$ = number of reads, $L$ = read length, $G$ = genome size | Fold coverage (×) |
| **GC Content** | $\text{GC\%} = \frac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100$ | Fraction of guanine + cytosine bases | Percentage |
| **Lander-Waterman Model** | $C = N \cdot L / G$ | Expected coverage (same as mean depth formula) | Fold |
| **Probability of Coverage Gap** | $P(\text{no read at position}) = e^{-C}$ | For a Poisson model of random sampling | Probability (0–1) |
| **Throughput** | $T = N \times L$ | Total bases generated per run | Bases (b, kb, Mb, Gb) |
| **Cost per Megabase** | $\text{Cost/Mb} = \frac{\text{Run Cost}}{T \text{ in Mb}}$ | Economic efficiency metric | USD / Mb |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Cell Rule:** Whenever you write a mathematical vertical bar in your own KTU exam answers (e.g., for absolute value), use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX — the raw pipe `|` will break your markdown table rendering.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

DNA sequencing lies at the intersection of **biomedical engineering, computer science, and data analytics**:

- **Bioinformatics Pipelines:** FASTQ → BAM → VCF (variant call format) workflow
- **Cloud Genomics:** AWS, Google Cloud provide on-demand sequencing compute (e.g., **Illumina BaseSpace Sequence Hub**)
- **Edge Devices:** Oxford Nanopore MinION enables **field-based pathogen surveillance** (used during the **Ebola outbreak (2014–2016)** and **COVID-19 genomic surveillance**)
- **AI-Assisted Base Calling:** Deep learning models (e.g., **Google DeepVariant**, **Oxford Nanopore Guppy**) achieve >**Q30 accuracy** from raw electrical signals

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Phred Quality Score from Base-Calling Error Probability

The **Phred score** is the foundation of sequencing quality assessment. Let us derive the relationship between quality score and base-calling accuracy.

**Given:**
- $P$ = probability of an incorrect base call (error probability)
- $Q$ = Phred quality score

**Step 1 — Define the logarithmic relationship between Q and P:**

The Phred quality score is defined as ten times the negative base-10 logarithm of the error probability:

$$
Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the error probability P:**

Rearranging the logarithmic equation to express $P$ in terms of $Q$:

$$
\log_{10}(P) = -\frac{Q}{10}
$$

Applying the exponential function (base 10) to both sides:

$$
P = 10^{-Q/10}
$$

**Step 3 — Express base-calling accuracy:**

The accuracy is one minus the error probability:

$$
\text{Accuracy} = 1 - P = 1 - 10^{-Q/10}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example for Q = 20:**

$$
P = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 = 99\%
$$

**Step 5 — Worked example for Q = 30:**

$$
P = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001
$$

$$
\text{Accuracy} = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999 = 99.9\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Illumina platforms typically report **Q30+ for >80% of bases** in a standard run, which is why Q30 is the industry benchmark for **high-quality clinical sequencing**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Coverage Depth (Lander-Waterman Model)

**Given:**
- $G$ = genome size (in base pairs)
- $N$ = number of reads produced
- $L$ = average read length

**Step 1 — Total bases sequenced:**

Each read contributes $L$ bases, and there are $N$ reads:

$$
T = N \times L
$$

**Step 2 — Mean coverage (number of times each base is sampled on average):**

Coverage is the ratio of total sequenced bases to genome size:

$$
C = \frac{T}{G} = \frac{N \times L}{G}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example:**

Suppose we sequence the **E. coli genome** ($G = 4.6 \times 10^6$ bp) with **Illumina MiSeq**, producing $N = 2 \times 10^6$ reads of length $L = 250$ bp.

$$
T = (2 \times 10^6) \times 250 = 5 \times 10^8 \text{ bases}
$$

$$
C = \frac{5 \times 10^8}{4.6 \times 10^6} \approx 108.7 \times \text{ coverage}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability that a given position is NOT covered:**

Assuming random (Poisson) sampling, the probability of zero reads covering any given position is:

$$
P(\text{gap at position}) = e^{-C} = e^{-108.7} \approx 0
$$

For lower coverage (e.g., $C = 5\times$):

$$
P(\text{gap}) = e^{-5} \approx 0.0067 \approx 0.67\%
$$

This is why clinical **whole-genome sequencing typically requires ≥30× coverage** to ensure virtually complete coverage.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sanger Sequencing Fragment Electrophoresis Resolution

**Given:**
- Voltage across capillary: $V$
- Capillary length: $\ell$
- Electric field: $E = V/\ell$
- Electrophoretic mobility of fragment of length $L$: $\mu(L) = \mu_0 / L$

**Step 1 — Migration velocity:**

The velocity of a fragment under the applied field is:

$$
v(L) = E \cdot \mu(L) = \frac{V}{\ell} \cdot \frac{\mu_0}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Time to traverse the capillary:**

$$
t(L) = \frac{\ell}{v(L)} = \frac{\ell^2}{V \cdot \mu_0 / L} = \frac{\ell^2 \cdot L}{V \cdot \mu_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Resolution between two adjacent fragment lengths:**

For two fragments differing by 1 nucleotide (length $L$ and $L+1$):

$$
\Delta t = \frac{\ell^2}{V \cdot \mu_0}
$$

This is independent of $L$ at first order, but in practice resolution decreases for very long fragments due to **band broadening (diffusion)**. Modern capillary arrays achieve single-nucleotide resolution up to **~1000 bp per read**.

---

### 3.4 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sequencing Technologies

| Parameter | Sanger | Illumina (NGS) | PacBio | Oxford Nanopore |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Generation** | 1st | 2nd | 3rd | 3rd |
| **Read Length** | 500–1000 bp | 50–300 bp (PE) | 10,000–25,000 bp | 1,000–>1,000,000 bp |
| **Throughput / Run** | ~0.5 Mb | 100–3000 Gb | 10–30 Gb | 1–50 Gb |
| **Accuracy** | >99.99% (Q40) | >99.9% (Q30) | >99.9% (HiFi) | ~99% (raw), >99.9% (consensus) |
| **Cost per Mb (approx.)** | ~$500 | ~$0.01 | ~$0.10 | ~$0.50 |
| **Run Time** | 0.5–3 hours | 1–3 days | 0.5–2 days | Minutes–48 hours |
| **Best Use Case** | Single-gene validation | Whole-exome / whole-genome | Structural variants, isoform sequencing | Field diagnostics, ultra-long assembly |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sanger Sequencing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Double-Stranded DNA Template] --> B[Step 1: Heat Denaturation at 95C]
    B --> C[Step 2: Primer Annealing at 55C]
    C --> D[Step 3: Extension with DNA Polymerase]
    D --> E{DdNTP Incorporated?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Chain Termination at Position N]
    E -->|No| G[Continue Extension]
    G --> E
    F --> H[Step 4: Capillary Gel Electrophoresis]
    H --> I[Step 5: Laser Fluorescence Detection]
    I --> J[Step 6: Software Base Calling]
    J --> K[Final Sequence Read ATGC Order]
```

### 4.2 Illumina NGS Sequencing-by-Synthesis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Genomic DNA] --> B[Fragmentation to 200 to 500 bp]
    B --> C[Adapter Ligation Library Prep]
    C --> D[Bind to Flow Cell Surface]
    D --> E[Bridge Amplification Cluster Generation]
    E --> F[Denature and Linearize Strands]
    F --> G[Add Fluorescent Reversible Terminators]
    G --> H[Laser Excitation and Image Capture]
    H --> I[Base Calling via Color Detection]
    I --> J[FASTQ Output File Generated]
    J --> K[BWA Alignment to Reference Genome]
    K --> L[Variant Calling GATK or DeepVariant]
    L --> M[VCF Report Pathogenic Variants]
```

### 4.3 Nanopore Sequencing Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DNA Fragment with Motor Protein] --> B[Biological Nanopore Embedded in Membrane]
    B --> C[Applied Voltage Creates Ion Current]
    C --> D[Current Signal Dwell Time per Kmer]
    D --> E[Base Calling via Deep Learning Model]
    E --> F[Real Time Sequence Output FAST5 or FASTQ]
    F --> G[Downstream Analysis Alignment Assembly]
```

### 4.4 DNA Sequencing Generational Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y1977A[Sanger 1977] --> Y1977B[Maxam Gilbert 1977]
    Y1977B --> Y2003[Human Genome Project 2003]
    Y2003 --> Y2005[Illumina NGS 2005]
    Y2005 --> Y2010[PacBio SMRT 2010]
    Y2010 --> Y2012[Oxford Nanopore MinION 2012]
    Y2012 --> Y2020[Single Cell Spatially Resolved 2020s]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `Y1977A`, `A`, `B`) to comply with Mermaid's syntax rules. Labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting to ensure reliable rendering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define DNA sequencing. List any two applications of DNA sequencing in medical diagnostics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DNA sequencing is the laboratory technique used to determine the precise linear order of nucleotides (Adenine, Thymine, Cytosine, Guanine) in a DNA molecule **(1 mark)**. Two applications in medical diagnostics are: **(i)** identification of disease-causing genetic mutations such as BRCA1/BRCA2 for hereditary breast cancer risk **(1 mark)**, and **(ii)** pathogen identification and surveillance, e.g., tracking SARS-CoV-2 variants through whole-genome sequencing **(1 mark)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*What is the Phred quality score? If a base has Q = 40, calculate the probability that it is called incorrectly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Phred quality score is a logarithmic measure of base-calling accuracy used in DNA sequencing, defined as $Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$, where $P$ is the error probability **(1 mark)**. For $Q = 40$:

$$
P = 10^{-Q/10} = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001
$$

**(1 mark)**. Thus, the probability of an incorrect base call is **0.0001 (0.01%)** **(1 mark)**, corresponding to **99.99% accuracy**.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE full question (with both sub-parts)** as per the KTU 2024 internal choice rule.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Sanger Sequencing Deep Dive

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the principle and steps of **Sanger's chain termination method** of DNA sequencing with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Sanger sequencing with **Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)** in terms of throughput, read length, cost, and clinical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3(A)(a) — 7 marks

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Sanger sequencing uses **2',3'-dideoxynucleotides (ddNTPs)** that lack a 3'-OH group. When a ddNTP is incorporated into the growing DNA chain, no further phosphodiester bond can form — the chain is **terminated**. By running four parallel reactions (one per ddNTP), a complete set of fragments is generated, and electrophoresis reveals the sequence.

2. **Steps (4 marks):**
   - **Denaturation:** DNA template heated to ~95°C to separate strands
   - **Primer Annealing:** A short primer hybridizes to the template at ~55°C
   - **Extension & Termination:** DNA polymerase + dNTPs + labeled ddNTPs; synthesis stops randomly at each A, T, G, or C
   - **Electrophoresis & Detection:** Capillary electrophoresis separates fragments; laser detects fluorescent labels; software reads the colored ladder
   - **Sequence Output:** Software assembles the ordered bases into a final read

3. **Diagram (1 mark):** Use the Sanger workflow diagram from Section 4.1.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3(A)(b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | Sanger (1st Gen) | Illumina NGS (2nd Gen) | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput** | ~0.5 Mb per run | 100–3000 Gb per run | 2 |
| **Read Length** | 500–1000 bp | 50–300 bp (paired-end) | 2 |
| **Cost per Base** | High (~$0.50/base) | Very low (~$0.000001/base) | 1.5 |
| **Clinical Use** | Single-gene validation, confirmatory testing | Whole-exome, whole-genome, multi-gene panels, NIPT | 1.5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse ddNTPs (dideoxy — chain terminator) with dNTPs (deoxy — normal building block). Many students lose marks by mixing these up.
> - Always specify **fluorescent labeling** in modern Sanger sequencing (single-tube reaction with 4-color ddNTPs), not radioactive labeling (that was the original 1977 method).
> - In comparison questions, mention at least **4 distinct parameters** and give **specific numerical values** — vague answers like "NGS is faster" earn partial credit only.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — NGS and Coverage Analysis

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **Illumina Sequencing-by-Synthesis (SBS)** workflow. Explain the role of **bridge amplification** and **reversible terminators**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clinical lab sequences a bacterial genome of size **5.2 Mb** using an Illumina platform. The run produces **$4 \times 10^6$** paired-end reads, each of length **150 bp**. Calculate the **mean coverage depth**. If the run reports that **75% of bases** have Phred score Q30 or higher, calculate the **error probability** for those bases. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3(B)(a) — 7 marks

1. **Library Preparation (1.5 marks):** Genomic DNA is fragmented (200–500 bp), ends are repaired, and platform-specific **adapter oligos** are ligated to both ends.

2. **Bridge Amplification (2.5 marks):** Adapter-ligated fragments bind to a flow cell coated with complementary oligos. The fragment bends over, and its other adapter binds a nearby oligo — forming a "bridge." PCR amplification creates a **clonal cluster** of ~1000 identical copies, enabling a strong enough fluorescent signal for detection.

3. **Sequencing-by-Synthesis with Reversible Terminators (2.5 marks):** DNA polymerase adds one fluorescently-labeled dNTP at a time. The 3'-OH is chemically **blocked**, ensuring only one base is added per cycle. After imaging, the **blocker and fluorophore are chemically cleaved**, allowing the next cycle. Each of the 4 nucleotides has a **distinct color**.

4. **Base Calling & Output (0.5 marks):** Images are processed into FASTQ files; reads are aligned to a reference genome.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3(B)(b) — 7 marks

**Part 1 — Coverage Calculation (4 marks):**

Total bases sequenced (using the formula $T = N \times L$):
$$
T = (4 \times 10^6) \times 150 = 6 \times 10^8 \text{ bases}
$$

Mean coverage depth ($C = T / G$):
$$
C = \frac{6 \times 10^8}{5.2 \times 10^6} \approx 115.38 \times
$$

**Answer: Mean coverage ≈ 115.4×** — this is more than sufficient for clinical-grade variant calling.

**Part 2 — Error Probability at Q30 (3 marks):**

Given $Q = 30$, apply the Phred formula:
$$
P = 10^{-Q/10} = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001
$$

**Answer: Error probability = 0.001 (0.1%)** — corresponding to **99.9% base-calling accuracy**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - In paired-end sequencing, each fragment produces **TWO reads** — but for total-base calculations, count **both reads** (i.e., $N = 4 \times 10^6$ pairs means $8 \times 10^6$ total reads). The above solution treats $N$ as total reads; always **read the problem carefully**.
> - Always **carry units** (bp, Mb, Gb) and **report coverage in "×"** notation.
> - For Q30, memorize that accuracy = **99.9%** and error probability = **1 in 1000** — this is a frequently tested constant.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **DNA sequencing** reads the exact order of **A, T, C, G** nucleotides in a DNA molecule
- ✅ **Sanger sequencing** = chain termination using **ddNTPs**; gold standard for validation; 500–1000 bp reads
- ✅ **Maxam-Gilbert** = chemical cleavage at specific bases; now largely obsolete
- ✅ **NGS (Illumina)** = sequencing-by-synthesis with **bridge amplification** and **reversible terminators**; massive parallelism; short reads (50–300 bp)
- ✅ **PacBio SMRT** = long-read, single-molecule, real-time sequencing
- ✅ **Oxford Nanopore** = detects bases by changes in **electrical current** as DNA passes through a pore; ultra-long reads; portable (MinION)
- ✅ **Phred score formula:** $Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$; **Q30 = 99.9% accuracy** is the clinical benchmark
- ✅ **Mean coverage formula:** $C = (N \times L) / G$; clinical WGS typically requires **≥30× coverage**
- ✅ **Clinical applications:** disease diagnosis, pharmacogenomics, NIPT, infectious disease tracking, forensics
- ✅ **Workflow:** DNA extraction → Library preparation → Sequencing → Base calling → Alignment → Variant calling → Clinical interpretation
- ✅ **Key file formats:** **FASTQ** (raw reads + quality), **BAM/SAM** (aligned reads), **VCF** (variant calls), **FASTA** (reference sequences)
- ✅ **Data explosion:** A single whole human genome at 30× coverage produces **~120 GB** of raw FASTQ data — driving demand for **cloud genomics** and **AI-powered base callers**

---

> **End of Module 4 — DNA Sequencing Notes**

topics.title: Transcriptomics and DNA Microarrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT866 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transcriptomics and DNA Microarrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transcriptomics — Formal Academic Definition

**Transcriptomics** is the comprehensive, high-throughput omics discipline concerned with the qualitative and quantitative study of the entire set of RNA molecules — collectively termed the **transcriptome** — that are transcribed from the genomic DNA of a cell, tissue, or organism at a given developmental stage, physiological condition, or pathological state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transcriptome = the complete catalogue of RNA transcripts (mRNA, tRNA, rRNA, miRNA, lncRNA, and other non-coding RNAs) present in a biological sample at a specific moment.

The central objectives of transcriptomics are to:
1. **Catalogue** every RNA species expressed.
2. **Quantify** expression levels (transcript abundance).
3. **Compare** expression profiles across conditions (e.g., healthy vs. diseased, treated vs. untreated).
4. **Map** transcript structures (5′/3′ UTRs, splice variants, TSSs).

### 1.2 DNA Microarray — Formal Academic Definition

A **DNA Microarray** (also called a **DNA chip**, **gene chip**, or **biochip**) is a solid substrate (typically a glass slide, silicon wafer, or nylon membrane) on which thousands to millions of known DNA **probes** (short oligonucleotides or longer cDNA fragments) are immobilised at precisely defined spatial coordinates, enabling simultaneous measurement of the expression of thousands of genes through the principle of **complementary base-pair hybridisation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (KTU Exam Favourite):** A microarray does **not** sequence nucleic acids. It is a **hybridisation-based quantification platform** — it measures *how much* of a specific transcript is present, not its linear order.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making it Intuitive

Imagine the **human genome (~3.2 billion base pairs, ~20,000 protein-coding genes)** as a vast library containing 20,000 unique books. At any given moment, a cell "reads" only a subset of these books (the *expressed* genes). The **transcriptome** is the collection of "open books" at a particular instant.

- A **DNA microarray** is like laying out 20,000 sticky notes (probes), one for each book title, on a giant board.
- You then take a **molecular "photocopy"** of all the open books (convert mRNA → fluorescently labelled cDNA) and pour this "ink" over the board.
- Books that were "open" (highly expressed) leave a **bright, dark stain** on their corresponding sticky note.
- Books that were "closed" (silent) leave **no stain**.
- A scanner measures the stain intensity → you now know **which books were read and how often**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Block (Must Memorise for KTU)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Transcriptome** | Complete set of all RNA molecules in a cell/tissue at a given time |
| **Gene Expression Profiling** | Measurement of mRNA levels for thousands of genes simultaneously |
| **Probe** | A short, known DNA sequence immobilised on the array surface |
| **Target** | The fluorescently labelled sample nucleic acid hybridising to the probe |
| **Hybridisation** | Watson-Crick base pairing between probe and target (A-T, G-C) |
| **Cy3 / Cy5** | Cyanine fluorophores emitting green (570 nm) and red (670 nm) light |
| **Fold Change (FC)** | Ratio of expression between two conditions, usually expressed as **2^\|log₂R\|** |
| **Differentially Expressed Gene (DEG)** | A gene whose expression differs statistically between conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** Microarray fluorescence is conventionally reported in arbitrary units, and the differential expression ratio is computed as **log₂ (Cy5/Cy3)**. A value of **+1.0** = **2-fold up-regulation**, **−1.0** = **2-fold down-regulation**, and **0** = **no change**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Transcriptomics

#### 2.1.1 The Central Dogma Context

Transcription is the process by which information encoded in DNA is copied into messenger RNA (mRNA) by **RNA polymerase II**. The abundance of each mRNA species in a cell is governed by:

- **Transcriptional regulation** (promoter activity, transcription factors, enhancers/silencers).
- **Post-transcriptional regulation** (mRNA stability, miRNA-mediated degradation, alternative splicing).
- **Cell-type specificity** (hepatocyte vs. neuron vs. cardiomyocyte transcriptomes are vastly different).
- **Temporal dynamics** (circadian rhythms, cell-cycle phase, developmental stage).
- **Pathological state** (cancer cells show radically rewired transcriptomes — *the cancer transcriptome*).

#### 2.1.2 The Transcriptome is Dynamic, the Genome is Static

This is the most important conceptual distinction for KTU:

| Feature | Genome | Transcriptome |
|---|---|---|
| **Constancy** | Largely static within an individual | Highly dynamic (changes second-by-second) |
| **Size** | ~3.2 Gb in humans | Varies: ~10⁵–10⁶ distinct transcripts |
| **Composition** | All genes + intergenic regions | Only currently transcribed genes |
| **Measurement** | Genome sequencing | Microarray or RNA-Seq |
| **Clinical Use** | Genotype, rare variant detection | Disease biomarkers, pathway dysregulation |

### 2.2 Two Principal Microarray Platforms

#### 2.2.1 Two-Color (cDNA) Microarray

- **Probe type:** Long cDNA clones (500–5000 bp) or long oligonucleotides (50–80 mer).
- **Labelling:** Two samples labelled with **Cy3** (green) and **Cy5** (red), co-hybridised to the *same* array.
- **Output:** Competitive, two-channel ratio → log₂(Cy5/Cy3).
- **Strength:** Direct comparison on one slide → eliminates inter-array variability.
- **Weakness:** Dye bias (Cy5 is more susceptible to ozone degradation and incorporates less efficiently).

#### 2.2.2 One-Color (Oligonucleotide) Microarray — Affymetrix GeneChip

- **Probe type:** Short oligonucleotides (**25-mers**), with multiple probes per gene (**PM = perfect match, MM = mismatch**).
- **Labelling:** One sample per array; expression reported as absolute intensity.
- **Output:** Hybridisation intensity, typically summarised by the **RMA (Robust Multi-array Average)** algorithm.
- **Strength:** Reproducibility, well-characterised probe sets, mature statistical pipelines.
- **Weakness:** Requires prior sequence knowledge; no direct two-sample comparison.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The fundamental design difference is **cDNA arrays (long probes, two-colour, competitive)** vs. **oligonucleotide arrays (short probes, one-colour, PM/MM design)**. The Affymetrix platform introduced the **PM/MM subtraction model** to correct for non-specific hybridisation: $I_{\text{corrected}} = I_{PM} - I_{MM}$. Modern RMA pipelines, however, use only PM probes and model background via a probabilistic model.

### 2.3 The Six-Step Microarray Workflow (Must Memorise)

1. **Sample Collection & RNA Extraction** — Isolate high-purity total RNA (TRIzol, column-based kits). Assess integrity via **RIN (RNA Integrity Number) ≥ 7**.
2. **Reverse Transcription → cDNA** — Reverse transcriptase converts mRNA → cDNA. For two-colour arrays, incorporate Cy3-dUTP/Cy5-dUTP during this step.
3. **Labelling & Purification** — Fluorescent nucleotide incorporation; remove unincorporated dyes.
4. **Hybridisation** — Labelled cDNA incubated with the array (16–24 h, 42–65 °C, hybridisation buffer with formamide/SSC).
5. **Washing** — Stringency washes (low to high SSC) to remove non-specific binding.
6. **Scanning & Image Analysis** — Confocal laser scanner excites Cy3 (532 nm laser) and Cy5 (635 nm laser); emitted fluorescence captured at 570 nm and 670 nm respectively. Software (e.g., GenePix, Feature Extraction) quantifies spot intensities.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Log₂ Expression Ratio** | $\log_2 R = \log_2\left(\frac{I_{Cy5}}{I_{Cy3}}\right)$ | $I$ = fluorescence intensity (a.u.) | $>0$ → up; $<0$ → down |
| 2 | **Fold Change** | $FC = 2^{\vert \log_2 R \vert}$ | Dimensionless | E.g., $\log_2 R = 2 \Rightarrow FC = 4$ |
| 3 | **PM-MM Model** | $I_{\text{corrected}} = I_{PM} - I_{MM}$ | a.u. | Affymetrix-specific |
| 4 | **Background Subtraction** | $I_{\text{net}} = I_{\text{spot}} - I_{\text{local bkg}}$ | a.u. | Computed per spot |
| 5 | **Z-score Normalisation** | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $x_i$ = intensity, $\mu,\sigma$ = array mean/SD | Row-wise across arrays |
| 6 | **Quantile Normalisation** | Rank-based, equalises distributions | — | Gold standard for Affymetrix |
| 7 | **Tukey's Biweight (RMA)** | $I_{\text{RMA}} = \text{Tukey\_biweight}(\log_2 PM)$ | log₂ a.u. | Robust to outliers |
| 8 | **Hybridisation Affinity Proxy** | $T_m \approx 64.9 + 41 \cdot \frac{(G+C-16.4)}{N}$ | $T_m$ in °C, $N$ = oligo length | Wallace rule for short oligos |
| 9 | **Bonferroni Correction** | $\alpha_{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{\alpha}{m}$ | $m$ = number of tests | For 20,000 genes: $\alpha = 0.05/20000 = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 10 | **FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg)** | $p_{(i)} \le \dfrac{i}{m} \cdot q$ | $q$ = acceptable FDR | Less conservative than Bonferroni |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Formulas 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, and 9 appear most frequently in KTU University Exam Part A and Part B questions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility

- **Oncology:** Identifying tumour-specific gene expression signatures (e.g., the 70-gene **MammaPrint** signature for breast cancer prognosis).
- **Pharmacogenomics:** Predicting drug response (e.g., HER2 expression testing guiding trastuzumab therapy).
- **Infectious Disease:** Pathogen detection and host-response profiling.
- **Toxicology:** The **ToxCast/Tox21** programs use microarrays to classify chemical toxicity.
- **Personalised Medicine:** Companion diagnostics — microarray-based tests now FDA-approved for multiple cancer subtypes.
- **Agricultural Biotechnology:** Stress-response transcriptomics in crops.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Fluorescence Intensity to Biological Interpretation

**Problem:** A cDNA microarray spot corresponding to Gene X shows Cy3 intensity = 800 a.u. and Cy5 intensity = 3200 a.u. (Cy3 = control/healthy, Cy5 = tumour). The local background for the spot is 50 a.u. Determine the log₂ ratio, fold change, and biological interpretation.

#### Step 1 — Background Subtraction

We must subtract local background from raw spot intensity to obtain the **net signal**. This corrects for non-specific fluorescence contributed by the slide surface, hybridisation buffer residues, and unbound dye.

$$
I_{\text{net, Cy3}} = I_{\text{raw, Cy3}} - I_{\text{bkg}} = 800 - 50 = 750 \; \text{a.u.}
$$

$$
I_{\text{net, Cy5}} = I_{\text{raw, Cy5}} - I_{\text{bkg}} = 3200 - 50 = 3150 \; \text{a.u.}
$$

**Logic:** Background is a constant additive noise floor; it does not represent hybridised target and must be removed before any ratio calculation.

#### Step 2 — Logarithmic Ratio Computation

We use the **logarithmic ratio** (base 2) rather than the raw linear ratio for two reasons:
1. It symmetrises up- and down-regulation (a 2-fold induction and a 2-fold repression become equal in magnitude but opposite in sign).
2. Variance stabilisation — logarithmic transformation approximates normality, satisfying assumptions of t-tests and ANOVA used downstream.

$$
\log_2 R \;=\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{I_{\text{net, Cy5}}}{I_{\text{net, Cy3}}}\right) \;=\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{3150}{750}\right) \;=\; \log_2(4.20)
$$

$$
\log_2(4.20) \;=\; \frac{\ln(4.20)}{\ln(2)} \;=\; \frac{1.4351}{0.6931} \;\approx\; 2.07
$$

#### Step 3 — Fold Change (Magnitude of Change)

The fold change is the **absolute magnitude** of differential expression, irrespective of direction.

$$
FC \;=\; 2^{\,\vert \log_2 R \vert} \;=\; 2^{\,\vert 2.07 \vert} \;=\; 2^{2.07} \;\approx\; 4.20
$$

**Cross-check:** Since the raw intensity ratio was 4.20, the fold change must equal 4.20 — the calculation is internally consistent. ✓

#### Step 4 — Biological Interpretation

- The gene is **up-regulated in tumour relative to control** because $\log_2 R = +2.07 > 0$ and $FC \approx 4.20$.
- A typical KTU / literature threshold for biological significance is $\vert \log_2 R \vert \geq 1.0$ (i.e., $\geq 2$-fold change). Here the change is far above threshold.
- A statistical test (e.g., moderated t-test with Benjamini-Hochberg correction) would be required to confirm significance; we assume $p_{adj} < 0.05$ for this worked example.

#### Step 5 — Decision Matrix for Microarray Data Interpretation

| $\log_2 R$ Range | Fold Change Range | Direction | Typical KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\log_2 R \geq +1$ | $FC \geq 2$ | Up-regulated | Gene is more active in test condition |
| $-1 < \log_2 R < +1$ | $FC < 2$ | No meaningful change | Biologically uninteresting |
| $\log_2 R \leq -1$ | $FC \geq 2$ | Down-regulated | Gene is repressed in test condition |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Algorithmic Derivation

We now derive the mathematical relationship between three normalisation approaches and show why **quantile normalisation** is the gold standard.

#### A) Z-Score Normalisation

Assumes Gaussian-distributed intensities. Each array $j$ is transformed to zero mean, unit variance:

$$
z_{ij} \;=\; \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}
$$

where $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ and $\sigma_j = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \mu_j)^2}$.

**Limitation:** Sensitive to outliers; assumes normality (often violated in raw intensity data).

#### B) Quantile Normalisation (Bolstad et al., 2003)

Step-by-step derivation of the algorithm:

1. **Step 1 — Sort each column** of the $n \times m$ intensity matrix (rows = probes, columns = arrays) in ascending order.
2. **Step 2 — Compute the row-wise mean** of the sorted columns. This produces a reference distribution $r_i$ for each rank $i$:

$$
r_i \;=\; \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{(i),j}^{\text{sorted}}
$$

3. **Step 3 — Assign** $r_i$ to the $i$-th ranked value of every column.
4. **Step 4 — Permute** the values back to their original probe order using the stored ranking permutation.

**Result:** All arrays now have *identical* empirical intensity distributions — eliminating systematic between-array distributional differences while preserving the *rank order* within each array (and thus the biological signal).

**Why superior to Z-score:** Robust to outliers, no distributional assumption, empirically data-driven.

#### C) RMA (Robust Multi-array Average) — The End-to-End Pipeline

RMA combines three sequential steps:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{RMA} \;=\; \text{Background correction (PM-only, model-based)} \;+\; \text{Quantile normalisation} \;+\; \text{Median polish summarisation (Tukey biweight)}\;}
$$

The Tukey biweight summarisation step, for a probe set with $n$ probes:

$$
T_b \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i(u_i) \cdot u_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i(u_i)}
$$

where $u_i$ is the normalised log-intensity of probe $i$ and $w_i(u_i)$ is the biweight weight function:

$$
w_i(u_i) \;=\; \begin{cases} \left(1 - u_i^2\right)^2 & \text{if } \vert u_i \vert < 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

with $u_i = \frac{x_i - \text{median}}{\text{median of } \vert x_i - \text{median} \vert}$. This **down-weights outliers** while preserving the bulk of the signal — a critical property for probe-level summarisation across replicate probes.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Multiple Hypothesis Testing

**Problem:** A microarray experiment tests 20,000 genes. The experimenter uses an unadjusted significance threshold of $\alpha = 0.05$. How many false positives are expected by chance alone? Apply the Bonferroni correction and the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure at $q = 0.05$.

#### Bonferroni Expected False Positives (uncorrected)

$$
E(FP) \;=\; m \cdot \alpha \;=\; 20000 \times 0.05 \;=\; 1000 \text{ false positives}
$$

This is clearly unacceptable: 1,000 out of 20,000 genes would be "significant" purely by chance.

#### Bonferroni Corrected Threshold

$$
\alpha_{\text{adj}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{m} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{20000} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Any gene with $p < 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ is declared significant. **Conservative** — sacrifices statistical power.

#### Benjamini-Hochberg FDR (less conservative)

Sort p-values ascending: $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(m)}$. The largest $i$ satisfying:

$$
p_{(i)} \;\leq\; \frac{i}{m} \cdot q
$$

is the significance cutoff. Genes with rank $\leq i$ are declared significant, controlling the **False Discovery Rate** to $\leq 5\%$.

For our problem, the maximum allowed $p_{(i)}$ is:

$$
p_{\text{cutoff, max}} \;=\; \frac{i}{20000} \times 0.05
$$

This is always *less stringent* than Bonferroni, retaining more true positives while still controlling the *proportion* of false discoveries.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Complete Microarray Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Collection\nTissue or Cell Line] --> B[Total RNA Extraction\nTRIzol Column Kits]
    B --> C[Quality Check\nRIN >= 7 Bioanalyzer]
    C --> D[mRNA Enrichment\nPoly T Selection]
    D --> E[Reverse Transcription\nGenerate cDNA]
    E --> F[Fluorescent Labelling\nCy3 dUTP and Cy5 dUTP]
    F --> G[Hybridisation Chamber\n16 to 24 Hours 42 to 65 C]
    G --> H[Stringency Washing\nRemove Non Specific Binding]
    H --> I[Confocal Laser Scanning\n532 nm and 635 nm]
    I --> J[Image Analysis Software\nGenePix Feature Extraction]
    J --> K[Background Subtraction and Normalisation\nRMA Quantile]
    K --> L[Statistical Analysis\nDifferential Expression]
    L --> M[Biological Interpretation\nPathways Networks]
    M --> N[Validation by qRT PCR]
```

**Architecture Description:** This block-level functional architecture maps the sequential data-flow from biological sample to biological insight. Each stage introduces a specific data transformation: **physical** (extraction), **chemical** (labelling), **biophysical** (hybridisation), **optical** (scanning), and **computational** (normalisation). The **feedback loop** from M → N is a KTU-favoured discussion point: microarrays have higher noise than qRT-PCR, so independent validation is mandatory for publication and clinical use.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Two-Colour vs. One-Colour Array — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TwoColor[Two Colour cDNA Microarray]
        S1[Sample A\nHealthy Tissue] --> RT1[Reverse Transcription]
        S2[Sample B\nTumour Tissue] --> RT1
        RT1 --> L1[Cy3 Labelled cDNA]
        RT1 --> L2[Cy5 Labelled cDNA]
        L1 --> H1[Co Hybridise\nSame Slide]
        L2 --> H1
        H1 --> SC1[Dual Laser Scan]
        SC1 --> R1[Compute log2 Cy5 by Cy3]
    end

    subgraph OneColor[One Colour Affymetrix Array]
        S3[Sample X] --> RT2[Reverse Transcription]
        RT2 --> L3[Biotin Labelled cRNA]
        L3 --> H2[Hybridise\nOne Sample per Array]
        H2 --> SC2[Streptavidin Phycoerythrin]
        SC2 --> SC3[Single Laser Scan 570 nm]
        SC3 --> R2[RMA Pipeline\nQuantile Normalisation]
    end

    R1 --> Out[Biological Conclusion]
    R2 --> Out
```

**Architecture Description:** This diagram contrasts the **competitive co-hybridisation** strategy of two-colour arrays (Sample A vs. Sample B on one physical slide, producing a *direct* ratio) with the **absolute intensity** strategy of one-colour Affymetrix GeneChips (one sample per slide, comparison done *across* arrays after statistical normalisation). The two designs are **mathematically equivalent** in their biological interpretation, but the normalisation requirements differ fundamentally.

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Hybridisation Principle — The Core Physical Chemistry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Immobilised Probe\nKnown DNA Sequence\n5 Prime to 3 Prime on Slide] -->|Complementary Base Pairing| T[Fluorescently Labelled Target cDNA from Sample]
    T -->|Excitation by 532 nm Laser| E[Emission at 570 nm Cy3 Green]
    T -->|Excitation by 635 nm Laser| F[Emission at 670 nm Cy5 Red]
    E --> Q[Quantified as Intensity per Spot]
    F --> Q
    Q --> Comp[Pixel by Pixel Comparison\nRed Green Yellow]
    Comp --> Result{Intensity Ratio\nGreater than 2 Fold}
    Result -->|Yes in Cy5| UP[Upregulated in Test Sample]
    Result -->|Yes in Cy3| DOWN[Upregulated in Reference]
    Result -->|Equal Yellow| NC[No Differential Expression]
```

**Architecture Description:** The flowchart captures the **decisional logic** of microarray interpretation. **Yellow** spots indicate equal Cy3 and Cy5 binding (no change). **Red** spots indicate higher Cy5 → up-regulation in the test sample. **Green** spots indicate higher Cy3 → up-regulation in the reference. This RGB-style "traffic-light" interpretation is a KTU viva-favourite conceptual question.

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Data-Analysis Decision Tree (From Raw Data to DEG List)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Raw[Raw CEL or GPR File] --> QC[Quality Control\nMA Plots Box Plots]
    QC --> Norm{Choose Normalisation}
    Norm -->|Two Colour| LO[LOWESS Normalisation]
    Norm -->|One Colour| QN[Quantile Normalisation]
    LO --> Stat[Linear Model Fit per Gene\nlimma Package]
    QN --> Stat
    Stat --> Test[Moderated t Test]
    Test --> Adj{Multiple Testing\nCorrection}
    Adj --> Bon[Bonferroni\nConservative]
    Adj --> BH[Benjamini Hochberg FDR\nStandard Choice]
    Bon --> List[DEG List]
    BH --> List
    List --> Cluster[Hierarchical or K Means Clustering]
    List |> Path[Pathway Enrichment\nGO KEGG]
    List --> Biomark[Biomarker Candidate]
    Cluster --> Heat[Heatmap Visualisation]
    Path --> Interp[Biological Insight]
    Biomark --> Interp
    Heat --> Interp
    Interp --> Validate[Validate by qRT PCR\nWestern Blot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Mermaid Syntax:** The above diagram uses the `|>` (right-pointing thick arrow) operator in Mermaid to indicate a parallel alternative branch from the DEG List node — this is a valid Mermaid edge syntax allowing one source node to feed multiple downstream analytical routes simultaneously.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define transcriptome. Differentiate between transcriptome and genome.**

**Model Answer:**

The **transcriptome** is the complete set of RNA transcripts (mRNA, tRNA, rRNA, miRNA, lncRNA) present in a cell or tissue at a given time and condition.

| Parameter | Genome | Transcriptome |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Entire DNA including introns & intergenic regions | Only transcribed RNA |
| Stability | Static within an individual | Highly dynamic |
| Size | ~3.2 Gb (human) | Varies (10⁵–10⁶ transcripts) |
| Methodology | DNA sequencing | Microarray, RNA-Seq |
| Information content | Potential functions | Actual functional activity |

*(Word limit target: ~80–100 words. 3 marks = 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the comparison table.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of hybridisation used in DNA microarrays.**

**Model Answer:**

A DNA microarray uses the principle of **complementary base pairing (Watson-Crick hybridisation)** between two single-stranded nucleic acids. Short, known DNA sequences (**probes**) are immobilised on a solid surface. Fluorescently labelled sample cDNA (**targets**) are washed over the array. If a target strand is complementary to a probe, a stable duplex is formed via hydrogen bonds (A=T, G≡C). Non-complementary strands are washed off. The fluorescence intensity at each spot is proportional to the amount of bound target, which in turn reflects the abundance of that transcript in the original sample. Two fluorophores (Cy3 = 570 nm emission, Cy5 = 670 nm emission) allow simultaneous two-colour competitive hybridisation.

*(~100 words. 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
> Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** on microarray questions by:
> 1. Confusing **probe** and **target** (probe is on the slide, target is in the sample).
> 2. Forgetting to subtract **background** before computing ratios.
> 3. Using the **linear ratio** instead of **log₂ ratio** when asked.
> 4. Not mentioning the **PM/MM design** for Affymetrix — examiners allocate dedicated marks.
> 5. Omitting the **validation step (qRT-PCR)** in the workflow — clinical/biological translation is mandatory.
> 6. Mixing up **Cy3** (green) and **Cy5** (red) wavelengths.

---

#### **PART B — Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete workflow of a two-colour cDNA microarray experiment** from sample preparation to data interpretation. Mention all key reagents and instrumentation at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microarray spot for gene BRCA1 yields Cy3 (control) intensity = 400 a.u. and Cy5 (tumour) intensity = 2400 a.u. with local background = 80 a.u. **Compute** the log₂ ratio, the fold change, and **interpret** the biological significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q. A (a):**

The **two-colour cDNA microarray workflow** consists of six sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Sample Collection and RNA Extraction (1 mark)**
- Source: tumour tissue and matched healthy control (same patient, paired design preferred).
- Extraction: TRIzol reagent (guanidinium thiocyanate–phenol–chloroform) or column-based kits (Qiagen RNeasy).
- Quality check: Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer, **RIN ≥ 7** mandatory; $A_{260}/A_{280} \geq 1.8$.

**Stage 2 — mRNA Enrichment and Reverse Transcription (1 mark)**
- mRNA isolation via **oligo-dT chromatography** (poly-A tail selection).
- Reverse transcription: MMLV reverse transcriptase, primers (oligo-dT or random hexamers), incorporating **Cy3-dUTP** (control) and **Cy5-dUTP** (tumour) — these are fluorescent nucleotide analogues incorporated into the nascent cDNA.

**Stage 3 — Hybridisation (1 mark)**
- Cy3-cDNA and Cy5-cDNA are **mixed, denatured (95 °C, 2 min)**, and applied to the microarray slide under a coverslip.
- Hybridisation in a humidified chamber at **42–65 °C for 16–24 hours** in buffer containing SSC, Denhardt's solution, SDS, and formamide (reduces $T_m$).
- Competitive binding: each probe spot competes for binding of both labelled cDNA populations.

**Stage 4 — Washing (1 mark)**
- Stringency washes with decreasing SSC concentration (2× → 0.1×) and SDS to remove non-specific and weakly bound targets.
- Final rinse in 100% ethanol, air-dry.

**Stage 5 — Scanning (1 mark)**
- Confocal laser scanner (e.g., GenePix 4000B, Agilent).
- **532 nm laser excites Cy3** → emission captured at **570 nm (green channel)**.
- **635 nm laser excites Cy5** → emission captured at **670 nm (red channel)**.
- Two 16-bit TIFF images generated.

**Stage 6 — Image Analysis, Normalisation, and Interpretation (2 marks)**
- Software: GenePix Pro, SpotFinder — automated grid alignment, spot segmentation, intensity quantification.
- **Background subtraction:** $I_{\text{net}} = I_{\text{spot}} - I_{\text{bkg}}$.
- **Normalization:** **LOESS (locally weighted scatterplot smoothing)** applied to the **MA-plot** (M = log ratio, A = mean log intensity) to remove intensity-dependent dye bias.
- **Statistical analysis:** linear model fit per gene (limma package in R/Bioconductor), moderated t-test, Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction.
- **Biological interpretation:** Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment, KEGG pathway analysis, hierarchical clustering visualisation (heatmap), principal component analysis (PCA).

**Block Diagram (described verbally for examination):**
A clear labelled flowchart showing: Sample → RNA → cDNA+Cy3/Cy5 → Hybridise → Wash → Scan → Image → Normalise → Statistics → DEG list → Pathways → Validation (qRT-PCR). *(Examiner allocates 1 mark for labelled diagram; 6 marks for explanation.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q. A (b):**

**Step 1 — Background Subtraction:**

$$
I_{\text{net, Cy3}} = 400 - 80 = 320 \; \text{a.u.}
$$

$$
I_{\text{net, Cy5}} = 2400 - 80 = 2320 \; \text{a.u.}
$$

**Step 2 — Log₂ Ratio:**

$$
\log_2 R \;=\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{2320}{320}\right) \;=\; \log_2(7.25) \;=\; \frac{\ln 7.25}{\ln 2} \;\approx\; \frac{1.981}{0.6931} \;\approx\; 2.86
$$

**Step 3 — Fold Change:**

$$
FC \;=\; 2^{2.86} \;\approx\; 7.25
$$

**Step 4 — Biological Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
- BRCA1 is **up-regulated ~7.25-fold** in tumour tissue compared to healthy control ($\log_2 R = +2.86$).
- Since $FC \gg 2$ and $\vert \log_2 R \vert = 2.86 \gg 1$, this is a **biologically significant up-regulation**.
- Note: BRCA1 is canonically a **tumour suppressor**; in many breast/ovarian cancers it is *down-regulated* (loss-of-function). The result of this problem therefore suggests an atypical context (e.g., compensatory up-regulation in a BRCA1-wild-type tumour, or a synthetic lethality study) — the student should explicitly state the *direction* and *magnitude* without over-interpreting clinical meaning.
- Statistical significance (e.g., $p_{adj} < 0.05$) is required to confirm this is not noise.

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark background subtraction, 2 marks log ratio, 1 mark fold change, 3 marks interpretation. Total 7.)*

---

#### **PART B — Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Affymetrix GeneChip platform** in detail. Discuss the **PM-MM probe design**, hybridisation strategy, and the **RMA normalisation algorithm** with its mathematical steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **clinical applications of DNA microarrays**? Discuss at least **four applications** with one example each. Compare microarrays with **RNA-Seq** as alternative transcriptomics technologies. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q. B (a):**

**Affymetrix GeneChip — Overview (1 mark):**
The Affymetrix GeneChip is a **one-colour, oligonucleotide-based microarray** platform. Each gene is interrogated by a **probe set** of 11–20 pairs of 25-mer oligonucleotides synthesised *in situ* by photolithographic combinatorial chemistry on a quartz wafer.

**PM-MM Design (2 marks):**
- **Perfect Match (PM) probe:** 25-mer with sequence exactly complementary to the target transcript.
- **Mismatch (MM) probe:** Same 25-mer except the **13th (middle) base is substituted** with its complement (A↔T, G↔C). The MM probe measures **non-specific hybridisation** and cross-hybridisation noise.
- The original expression value was computed as $I = \log_2(PM - MM)$, but **modern RMA pipelines discard MM probes** because they were found to introduce more noise than signal correction.

**Hybridisation Strategy (1 mark):**
- One biotin-labelled cRNA (or cDNA) sample per array.
- Target is fragmented (~50–200 bp) and hybridised at **45 °C for 16 hours** in MES-containing buffer with BSA, herring sperm DNA, and DMSO.
- Post-hybridisation, **streptavidin–phycoerythrin (SAPE)** is added — the biotinylated cRNA binds SAPE, which provides the fluorescent signal (excited at 570 nm).

**RMA Algorithm — Three Mathematical Steps (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Model-Based Background Correction (PM-only):**
A probabilistic model assuming PM intensities are a mixture of true signal and background noise (Gaussian for signal, exponential for noise). Estimates the expected signal given the observed PM.

**Step 2 — Quantile Normalisation:**
Already derived in §3.2. Produces identical intensity distributions across all arrays in the experiment.

**Step 3 — Probe Set Summarisation via Median Polish:**
For each probe set with $n$ probes across multiple arrays, fits a linear model:

$$
\log_2(PM_{ij}) \;=\; \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}
$$

where $\mu$ = overall effect, $\alpha_i$ = probe effect, $\beta_j$ = array effect, $\epsilon_{ij}$ = residual. **Median polish** iteratively removes row and column medians to robustly estimate the overall expression $\mu$ for each probe set on each array. The result is a single **log₂ expression value per gene per array**.

---

**Model Solution to Q. B (b):**

**Clinical Applications of DNA Microarrays (4 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis** — **MammaPrint** (70-gene signature, Netherlands Cancer Institute) stratifies early-stage breast cancer patients into high-risk vs. low-risk for metastasis, guiding chemotherapy decisions. **FDA-approved.**

2. **Pharmacogenomics / Companion Diagnostics** — HER2/neu expression testing in breast cancer guides **trastuzumab (Herceptin)** therapy. AmpliChip CYP450 (Roche) tests for cytochrome P450 polymorphisms to predict drug metabolism.

3. **Cytogenetics / Copy Number Variation Detection** — **Array Comparative Genomic Hybridisation (aCGH)** detects chromosomal imbalances (microdeletions, microduplications) in prenatal diagnosis and intellectual disability. Higher resolution than karyotyping.

4. **Pathogen Detection and Typing** — Microarrays can simultaneously detect and serotype multiple pathogens (e.g., respiratory virus panel, mycobacterial species identification, HPV genotyping).

5. *(Bonus, optional)* **Forensic DNA analysis, SNP genotyping for GWAS, toxicogenomics.*

**Microarray vs. RNA-Seq (3 marks):**

| Feature | DNA Microarray | RNA-Seq |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Hybridisation to known probes | Direct sequencing (NGS) |
| **Sequence knowledge required** | Yes — must design probes for known genes | No — *de novo* discovery possible |
| **Dynamic range** | ~10²–10³ (limited by saturation, background) | >10⁵ (sequence-count based) |
| **Sensitivity** | Poor for low-abundance transcripts | High — can detect rare transcripts |
| **Discovery of novel transcripts/splice variants** | Limited | Excellent |
| **Quantification** | Relative (ratio-based) | Absolute (counts per million) |
| **Reproducibility** | High (mature pipelines) | High (improving) |
| **Cost per sample** | Lower | Higher (declining) |
| **Time** | Faster | Longer library prep + sequencing |
| **Data analysis complexity** | Moderate | High (alignment, assembly) |
| **Background noise** | Cross-hybridisation | Adapter contamination, GC bias |

**Conclusion for KTU exam:** RNA-Seq has largely **superseded microarrays** in research settings due to its superior dynamic range and unbiased transcript discovery, but **microarrays remain clinically dominant** in regulated diagnostic settings (e.g., MammaPrint, Oncotype DX) because of their **reproducibility, lower cost, simpler analysis, and established regulatory approval pathways**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ☐ **Transcriptome = all RNA in a cell at a given time; Genome = static DNA.** Differentiate confidently.
- ☐ **Microarray measures expression, not sequence.** It is **hybridisation-based**, not sequencing-based.
- ☐ **Two-colour (cDNA) vs. one-colour (Affymetrix).** Know the difference in design and output.
- ☐ **Cy3 = green = 570 nm emission; Cy5 = red = 670 nm emission.** Never confuse.
- ☐ **Six-step workflow:** Extract → RT → Label → Hybridise → Wash → Scan → Analyse.
- ☐ **Background subtraction is mandatory** before any ratio calculation.
- ☐ **Always use log₂ ratio**, not linear ratio, for differential expression.
- ☐ **Fold change = $2^{\vert \log_2 R \vert}$**, threshold typically $\geq 2$ for biological significance.
- ☐ **Affymetrix PM-MM model:** $I = PM - MM$ (historically); modern RMA uses PM-only.
- ☐ **RMA = Background correction + Quantile normalisation + Median polish.**
- ☐ **LOESS normalisation** for two-colour arrays (corrects intensity-dependent dye bias on MA-plot).
- ☐ **Bonferroni** = $\alpha/m$ (conservative). **Benjamini-Hochberg** = FDR control (standard for transcriptomics).
- ☐ **20,000 genes × α = 0.05 → 1,000 false positives by chance** — hence correction is essential.
- ☐ **Validation by qRT-PCR is mandatory** for any DEG claimed from a microarray study.
- ☐ **Hierarchical clustering and heatmaps** are the standard visualisation for differential expression.
- ☐ **GO and KEGG pathway enrichment** are the standard downstream biological interpretation.
- ☐ **Clinical applications:** MammaPrint (breast cancer prognosis), HER2 testing, aCGH, pharmacogenomics.
- ☐ **RNA-Seq has largely replaced microarrays** in research; microarrays remain in clinical diagnostics.
- ☐ **MammaPrint = 70-gene signature; Oncotype DX = 21-gene recurrence score** — both FDA-approved, KTU-favourite examples.
- ☐ **Major limitation of microarrays:** cross-hybridisation, limited dynamic range, requires prior sequence knowledge, cannot detect novel transcripts or splice variants.
- ☐ **Reverse transcriptase = MMLV (Moloney Murine Leukaemia Virus)** enzyme used in cDNA synthesis.
- ☐ **RIN ≥ 7** is the standard RNA quality threshold.
- ☐ **Photolithographic synthesis** = Affymetrix's in-situ oligonucleotide synthesis method (Fodor et al., 1991).
- ☐ **Hybridisation kinetics** depend on probe length, GC content, temperature, and salt concentration. Wallace rule for $T_m$ estimation of short oligos: $T_m \approx 64.9 + 41 \cdot \frac{(G+C-16.4)}{N}$ °C.

> **End of Notes — PEEBT866 Module 4: Transcriptomics and DNA Microarrays**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proteomics and Mass Spectrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT866 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Proteomics and Mass Spectrometer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Proteomics — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Proteomics** is the systematic, large-scale study of the entire protein complement (the **proteome**) of a biological system — including protein identification, quantification, structural characterization, post-translational modifications (PTMs), protein-protein interactions, and their functional states — at a given moment under defined physiological or pathological conditions. The term was coined in 1997 by **Marc Wilkins**, analogous to "genomics," but while the genome is largely static, the **proteome is highly dynamic** — it changes with cell type, developmental stage, disease state, and environmental stimulus. **Key Distinction:** | Property | Genome | Proteome | |---|---|---| | Composition | DNA sequence (4 bases) | 20 amino acids + PTMs | | Stability | Static across cell types | Dynamic, condition-dependent | | Size | Fixed (e.g., ~3.2 billion bp) | Variable (20,000+ proteins → >1,000,000 proteoforms) | | Modifications | None (epigenetics aside) | Phosphorylation, glycosylation, ubiquitination, etc. | ## 1.2 Mass Spectrometer — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Mass Spectrometer (MS)** is an analytical instrument that measures the **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** of ionized molecules. It produces a mass spectrum — a plot of ion signal intensity versus m/z — used to identify, quantify, and structurally elucidate chemical compounds and biomolecules (especially proteins and peptides). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of a Mass Spectrometer as a "Molecular Sorting Machine with a Weighing Scale."** > > Imagine a postal sorting facility: thousands of letters (molecules) of unknown origin arrive mixed together. The machine must: > 1. **Give each letter an address tag (ionize it)** — add an electric charge so it can be manipulated. > 2. **Weigh each letter extremely precisely (mass analysis)** — separate them by how "heavy" they are per unit charge. > 3. **Read the destination (detection)** — record where each one lands. > > In proteomics, proteins from a biological sample are first chopped into peptides (using an enzyme like **trypsin**), each peptide is "weighed" by the MS, and the resulting masses act as a **molecular fingerprint** to identify the parent protein from a database — much like fingerprint analysis identifies a person. > > **Why use MS for proteins?** Because proteins are far more complex and dynamic than DNA, and their **mass is an intrinsic, highly specific property** that reveals identity, structure, and modifications. ## 1.4 Core Metrics in Mass Spectrometry - **m/z (mass-to-charge ratio):** Dimensionless quantity; z = integer number of elementary charges. - **Resolution (R):** Ability to distinguish two adjacent mass peaks. - **Mass Accuracy:** Deviation of measured mass from theoretical mass (often in **ppm**). - **Sensitivity:** Lowest detectable amount (often **attomole, 10⁻¹⁸ mol**, level). - **Dynamic Range:** Up to **10⁵–10⁶** for modern instruments. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Proteomics Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Sample Preparation:** Biological tissue / fluid / cell lysate is collected. Proteins are extracted using lysis buffers containing detergents (e.g., **SDS, urea**), protease inhibitors, and reducing agents (e.g., **DTT**). 2. **Protein Separation:** Proteins are separated using: - **1D / 2D Gel Electrophoresis (2-DE):** Separates by isoelectric point (pI) and molecular weight. - **Liquid Chromatography (LC):** Reverse-phase LC, ion-exchange LC, or Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC) coupled directly to MS. 3. **Proteolytic Digestion:** Proteins are enzymatically cleaved into smaller, MS-friendly peptides using a **site-specific protease**, most commonly **trypsin** (cleaves at the C-terminal side of **Lys** and **Arg** residues, except when followed by Pro). 4. **Ionization:** Peptides are converted to gas-phase ions using "soft" ionization techniques — **Electrospray Ionization (ESI)** or **Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization (MALDI)**. Both were recognized by the **2002 Nobel Prize in Chemistry (Fenn & Tanaka)**. 5. **Mass Analysis:** Ions are separated by their m/z ratio using a mass analyzer (e.g., **TOF, quadrupole, ion trap, Orbitrap, FT-ICR**). 6. **Detection:** Ions striking the detector produce an electrical signal proportional to abundance. 7. **Data Analysis & Database Searching:** The experimental mass spectrum is compared against theoretical spectra generated from protein databases (**UniProt, NCBI nr**) using search engines like **MASCOT, SEQUEST, or MaxQuant**. ## 2.2 Sub-Disciplines of Proteomics - **Expression Proteomics:** Quantitative comparison of protein levels between samples (e.g., healthy vs. diseased). Tools: **SILAC, iTRAQ, TMT, Label-free quantification**. - **Structural Proteomics:** Determination of 3D protein structures and mapping of protein complexes. Tools: **Cryo-EM, X-ray crystallography, Hydrogen-Deuterium Exchange MS (HDX-MS)**. - **Functional Proteomics:** Study of protein function, activity, interactions, and signaling networks.
