- Complete linkage: No recombinants produced.
- Incomplete linkage: Recombinants produced due to **crossing over**.

**Concept of Crossing Over:**
- Physical exchange of segments between **non-sister chromatids** of homologous chromosomes during **Prophase I of Meiosis (Pachytene stage)**.
- Produces **recombinant chromosomes** carrying new combinations of alleles.

**Calculation:**

**Step 1: Identify Recombinants and Parentals**

| Offspring Type | Count | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| BbVg | 240 | Parental (non-recombinant) |
| bbvg | 260 | Parental (non-recombinant) |
| Bbvg | 240 | Recombinant |
| bbVg | 260 | Recombinant |

**Step 2: Total Recombinants**

$$\text{Recombinants} = 240 + 260 = 500$$

**Step 3: Total Offspring**

$$\text{Total} = 1000$$

**Step 4: Recombination Frequency**

$$RF = \frac{\text{Recombinants}}{\text{Total Offspring}} \times 100\%$$

$$RF = \frac{500}{1000} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Step 5: Map Distance**

$$\text{Map Distance} = RF = 50 \text{ cM}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**
- An RF of 50% indicates that genes **B** and **Vg** are **unlinked** (assorting independently) — they are located on **different chromosomes** OR are **very far apart** on the same chromosome (such that crossing over occurs in nearly every meiotic event).
- This is the **maximum observable recombination frequency** (RF cannot exceed 50% for two genes).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Examinations**
> 1. **Confusing Genotype with Phenotype:** Writing "genotype is Tall" — INCORRECT. Tall is the phenotype.
> 2. **Forgetting to Specify Generation:** Always write **P, F₁, F₂** explicitly in cross diagrams.
> 3. **Punnett Square Errors:** Drawing only 4 boxes for a dihybrid cross (should be 4×4 = 16 boxes).
> 4. **Forgetting the Unit cM:** Map distance must always be expressed in **centiMorgans (cM)**, not just as a percentage.
> 5. **Mixing up Parental vs. Recombinant:** In a test cross, the **largest two classes** are parental; the **smallest two classes** are recombinants.
> 6. **Not Explaining Mechanisms:** Always mention **Anaphase I** (for Law of Segregation) and **Prophase I/Pachytene** (for Crossing Over).
> 7. **Skipping Numerical Solutions:** Even if asked for a derivation, always show **every step** of the calculation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examination**

- **Mendel's Three Laws:** Dominance → Segregation → Independent Assortment.
- **Punnett Square Rule:** n genes → $2^n \times 2^n$ boxes; $2^n$ gamete types.
- **Phenotypic Ratios to Memorize:**
  - Monohybrid F₂ = **3:1**
  - Dihybrid F₂ = **9:3:3:1**
  - Test cross = **1:1** (for unlinked genes)
  - Incomplete dominance = **1:2:1** (phenotype = genotype)
- **Key Definitions:** Gene, Allele, Locus, Homozygous, Heterozygous, Dominant, Recessive, Genotype, Phenotype, Haploid, Diploid.
- **Morgan's Contributions:** Chromosomal theory, Sex-linkage, Linkage, Crossing Over.
- **Drosophila:** Eye color (white/red) was Morgan's landmark mutation; 2n = 8.
- **Crossing Over:** Occurs in **Pachytene** of Prophase I; produces **recombinants**.
- **Recombination Frequency Formula:** $RF = \frac{\text{Recombinants}}{\text{Total}} \times 100\%$
- **Map Distance:** $1\% \text{ RF} = 1 \text{ cM}$; **Maximum RF = 50%** (independent assortment).
- **Hardy-Weinberg Equation:** $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$ (5 assumptions must hold).
- **X-linked Recessive Pattern:** Affects **males more**; shows **criss-cross inheritance**.
- **Medical Relevance:** Foundation of genetic counseling, pedigree analysis, predictive testing, pharmacogenomics.
- **Famous Diseases:** Cystic fibrosis (AR), Huntington's (AD), Hemophilia A (XLR), Sickle cell anemia (AR with heterozygote advantage).
- **Year Memorization:** Mendel published in **1865**; Morgan's work began in **1910**.
- **P generation:** Parental; **F₁:** First filial; **F₂:** Second filial generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Points (Most Frequently Asked)**
> 1. Punnett square-based monohybrid and dihybrid cross problems.
> 2. Comparison of Mendel's and Morgan's experimental systems.
> 3. Recombination frequency calculation and gene mapping.
> 4. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium problems with numerical solving.
> 5. Sex-linked inheritance pedigree problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disease Gene Mapping in the Genomic Era
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT866 - MEDICAL INFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disease Gene Mapping in the Genomic Era

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disease Gene Mapping** is the systematic process of identifying the precise chromosomal locations and specific nucleotide sequence variants (alleles) that contribute to, predispose an individual to, or directly cause human diseases. In the genomic era, this process leverages high-throughput DNA sequencing, dense single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) arrays, and computational biology to correlate genomic loci with phenotypic disease outcomes across populations.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT866)**, disease gene mapping in the genomic era is broadly classified into two complementary analytical paradigms:

1. **Linkage Analysis** (family-based, rare-variant focused) — Tracks the co-segregation of genetic markers with disease phenotypes across pedigrees.
2. **Association Analysis** (population-based, common-variant focused) — Quantifies statistical correlation between alleles and disease status in unrelated individuals, exemplified by **Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS)**.

## 1.2 Key Genomic Era Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Locus (Loci)** | A fixed, specific physical position on a chromosome where a gene or genetic marker resides. |
| **Allele** | An alternative DNA sequence variant at a given locus. |
| **Genotype** | The pair of alleles an individual carries at a particular locus. |
| **Phenotype** | The observable expression of a genotype (e.g., diseased vs. healthy). |
| **Polymorphism** | A genomic variant where the minor allele frequency (MAF) is **≥ 1%** in a population. |
| **SNP** | Single Nucleotide Polymorphism — a single base-pair substitution; the most common type of human genetic variation. |
| **Haplotype** | A combination of alleles at multiple linked loci on a single chromosome that are inherited together. |
| **Penetrance** | The proportion of individuals carrying a particular genotype who also express the associated phenotype. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A disease gene map is essentially a coordinate system that links **genotype → phenotype** relationships, enabling precision medicine, predictive diagnostics, and therapeutic target identification.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Neighborhood Detective" Metaphor

Imagine a large city (the **human genome**, ~3.2 billion base pairs). Somewhere in this city, a "troublemaker" (the **disease allele**) is causing problems. You have two main strategies to find them:

- **Family-based Linkage (Pedigree Detective):** You interview relatives in a family. If the troublemaker always appears in family members who have the disease (and rarely in healthy relatives), you can triangulate the troublemaker's neighborhood by tracking which "street sign" (genetic marker) is co-inherited with the disease. This works best for **rare, highly penetrant** Mendelian disorders (e.g., Huntington's disease, Cystic Fibrosis).

- **Population-based Association (City-wide Census Detective):** You survey a large cross-section of the city. You compare the "street signs" (alleles) of 10,000 diseased individuals to 10,000 healthy controls. If a particular sign appears **disproportionately more often** in the diseased group, you've identified a hotspot. This is the basis of **GWAS** and works best for **common, complex diseases** (e.g., Type 2 Diabetes, Schizophrenia).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Disease Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Human Genome Size:** ~3.2 × 10⁹ base pairs (≈ **3.2 Gbp**)
> - **Estimated Gene Count:** ~**20,000–25,000** protein-coding genes
> - **Total SNPs in Human Genome:** > **10 million** (dbSNP database)
> - **GWAS Significance Threshold:** p-value < **5 × 10⁻⁸** (Bonferroni-corrected for ~1 million independent tests)
> - **Linkage Significance Threshold:** LOD score > **3.0** (odds of 1000:1 in favor of linkage)
> - **Linkage Disequilibrium Decay:** typically within **~50–500 kb** in outbred human populations
> - **Mendelian Inheritance in Man (OMIM) entries:** > **25,000** catalogued disease-gene relationships
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Strategic Pillars of Disease Gene Mapping

### Pillar 1: Linkage Analysis (Family-Based Mapping)

Linkage analysis exploits the principle of **genetic linkage** — the tendency of loci physically close on the same chromosome to be inherited together during meiosis (reduced recombination between them).

**Operational Workflow:**

1. **Pedigree Recruitment:** Collect multi-generational families with multiple affected and unaffected members.
2. **Marker Genotyping:** Type family members at polymorphic markers (microsatellites or SNPs).
3. **Recombination Counting:** Count recombination events between the marker and the disease locus.
4. **LOD Score Calculation:** Compute the statistical likelihood of linkage vs. no linkage.
5. **Locus Refinement:** Narrow the candidate interval through recombination mapping.
6. **Gene Identification:** Sequence candidate genes within the linked interval to identify causal mutations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Linkage Works:** Genetic distance is measured in **centimorgans (cM)**, where 1 cM corresponds to a recombination frequency of 1%. The closer two loci are, the less likely a crossover event will separate them, leading to co-segregation across generations.

### Pillar 2: Association Analysis (Population-Based Mapping)

Association analysis tests whether a particular allele occurs at a significantly different frequency in diseased vs. control populations. The modern implementation is the **Genome-Wide Association Study (GWAS)**.

**Operational Workflow:**

1. **Cohort Assembly:** Large, well-phenotyped case-control cohort (typically 1,000s to 100,000s of individuals).
2. **High-Density Genotyping:** Use SNP arrays (e.g., Illumina, Affymetrix) typing **> 500,000 to 2,000,000 SNPs** per individual.
3. **Imputation:** Statistical inference of untyped SNPs using reference haplotype panels (e.g., **1000 Genomes**, **Haplotype Reference Consortium (HRC)**).
4. **Statistical Testing:** Allele-wise or genotype-wise chi-square tests for each SNP.
5. **Multiple Testing Correction:** Apply Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg FDR methods.
6. **Replication:** Validate significant hits in independent cohorts.
7. **Fine-Mapping:** Use conditional analysis and functional genomics to pinpoint causal variants.
8. **Functional Validation:** CRISPR, eQTL analysis, reporter assays, and animal models.

## 2.2 The Concept of Linkage Disequilibrium (LD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linkage Disequilibrium (LD)** is the non-random association of alleles at two or more loci. In a disease-mapping context, if a marker allele is in strong LD with a disease allele, the marker "tags" the disease variant, allowing indirect association detection.

LD is the statistical engine that makes GWAS feasible: rather than typing every causal variant directly, we type a sparse set of "tag SNPs" that are correlated with nearby causal variants due to shared ancestral haplotypes.

**Key LD Visualizations:**
- **Haploview Plots:** Color-coded matrices showing pairwise D' or r² values.
- **LD Decay Plots:** r² vs. physical distance, showing exponential decline.

## 2.3 Mapping Resolution: Linkage vs. Association

| Parameter | Linkage Analysis | Association Analysis (GWAS) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Mapping Unit** | Centimorgans (cM) | Base pairs (bp) |
| **Resolution** | ~1–10 Mbp (coarse) | ~10–500 kbp (fine) |
| **Sample Type** | Multi-generational families | Unrelated case-control cohorts |
| **Variant Type Targeted** | Rare, high-penetrance | Common, low-penetrance |
| **Effect Size Detected** | Large (OR > 2) | Small (OR 1.1–1.5 typical) |
| **Number of Markers Required** | Few hundred microsatellites | Hundreds of thousands to millions of SNPs |
| **Statistical Threshold** | LOD > 3.0 | p < 5 × 10⁻⁸ |
| **Disease Examples** | Huntington's, BRCA1/2 breast cancer | Type 2 Diabetes, Crohn's, Schizophrenia |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Note:** All formulas below are core high-yield items frequently tested in PEEBT866 ESE.

### Recombination Frequency & Genetic Distance

$$
\theta = \frac{\text{Number of recombinant gametes}}{\text{Total number of gametes observed}}
$$

$$
d(\text{cM}) = 100 \times \theta \quad \text{(Haldane's mapping function for small } \theta \text{)}
$$

### LOD Score (Logarithm of Odds)

$$
\text{LOD}(\theta) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{L(\theta = \text{linked})}{L(\theta = 0.5)}\right)
$$

$$
\text{LOD Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{LOD}_i(\theta)
$$

**Decision Rule:** LOD ≥ **+3.0** → significant linkage; LOD ≤ **−2.0** → excludes linkage.

### Linkage Disequilibrium Coefficients

$$
D_{AB} = P_{AB} - p_A \cdot p_B
$$

$$
D' = \frac{D_{AB}}{D_{\max}}
$$

$$
r^2 = \frac{D_{AB}^2}{p_A \cdot p_B \cdot p_A' \cdot p_B'}
$$

where $p_A, p_B$ are allele frequencies and $P_{AB}$ is the haplotype frequency. Use $\vert D' \vert$ in calculations to avoid breaking tables.

### Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE)

$$
p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1
$$

where $p$ = frequency of major allele, $q$ = frequency of minor allele, $p + q = 1$.

### Chi-Square Test for Association (1 d.f. 2x2 Table)

$$
\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}
$$

### Odds Ratio (OR) for Case-Control Studies

$$
\text{OR} = \frac{a \cdot d}{b \cdot c}
$$

where $a$ = exposed cases, $b$ = exposed controls, $c$ = unexposed cases, $d$ = unexposed controls.

### Bonferroni Corrected Significance Threshold

$$
\alpha_{\text{Bonferroni}} = \frac{0.05}{N_{\text{tests}}}
$$

For a GWAS with 1,000,000 SNPs: $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-8}$.

### Effective Population Size & LD

$$
E[r^2] \approx \frac{1}{1 + 4N_e c}
$$

where $N_e$ = effective population size, $c$ = recombination fraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Disease gene mapping has direct applications in:
> - **Pharmacogenomics:** Predicting drug response (e.g., HLA-B*57:01 → Abacavir hypersensitivity).
> - **Polygenic Risk Scores (PRS):** Estimating lifetime disease risk from aggregate SNP effects.
> - **Companion Diagnostics:** FDA-approved gene tests (e.g., EGFR for lung cancer therapy).
> - **Gene Therapy Target Identification:** Locating causal genes for AAV or CRISPR delivery.
> - **Population Genetics & Forensics:** Disease prevalence in ancestral populations.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The LOD Score Statistic

The **LOD score** is the cornerstone of parametric linkage analysis. It compares the likelihood of observing pedigree data *given* a specific recombination fraction $\theta$ to the likelihood under the null hypothesis of no linkage ($\theta = 0.5$).

### Step 1: Define the Likelihood Ratio

Let $L(\theta)$ be the probability of observing the pedigree genotype data, given a recombination fraction $\theta$ between the marker and disease locus.

$$
\text{LR}(\theta) = \frac{L(\theta)}{L(\theta = 0.5)}
$$

**Logic:** The numerator evaluates the probability under linkage (recombination $< 50\%$). The denominator assumes independent assortment (no linkage, $\theta = 0.5$).

### Step 2: Take Base-10 Logarithm

To make computation additive across families and easier to interpret:

$$
\text{LOD}(\theta) = \log_{10}\left[\frac{L(\theta)}{L(\theta = 0.5)}\right]
$$

**Logic:** Logarithmic transformation makes the score additive across independent families, simplifying the joint analysis.

### Step 3: Maximum LOD Score (MLOD)

Find the $\theta$ that maximizes LOD:

$$
\hat{\theta} = \arg\max_{\theta} \text{LOD}(\theta)
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation Thresholds

$$
\text{Decision Rule:} \quad
\begin{cases}
\text{LOD} \geq 3.0 & \rightarrow \text{Significant linkage} \\
-2.0 \leq \text{LOD} < 3.0 & \rightarrow \text{Uncertain / Suggestive} \\
\text{LOD} \leq -2.0 & \rightarrow \text{Linkage excluded}
\end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** LOD = 3 corresponds to odds of 1000:1 in favor of linkage ($10^3$ = 1000). LOD = −2 corresponds to odds of 100:1 against linkage.

### Step 5: Numerical Example — Two-Point LOD

Suppose a pedigree yields the following likelihoods:

$$
L(\theta = 0.05) = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}, \quad L(\theta = 0.5) = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Compute the LOD:

$$
\text{LOD}(0.05) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = \log_{10}(50) = 1.699
$$

**Interpretation:** LOD = 1.699 < 3.0 → suggestive, not significant; more families or markers needed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Linkage Disequilibrium Coefficient D

### Step 1: Define Haplotype and Allele Frequencies

Consider two biallelic loci **A** (alleles $A_1, A_2$) and **B** (alleles $B_1, B_2$) with frequencies:

- $P_{A_1} = p_A$, $\quad P_{A_2} = p_A' = 1 - p_A$
- $P_{B_1} = p_B$, $\quad P_{B_2} = p_B' = 1 - p_B$

Let $P_{A_1 B_1} = P_{AB}$ be the observed haplotype frequency.

### Step 2: Expected Frequency Under Independence

Under random mating and linkage equilibrium:

$$
P_{AB}^{\text{expected}} = p_A \cdot p_B
$$

### Step 3: Define D

$$
D = P_{AB} - p_A \cdot p_B
$$

**Logic:** $D$ measures the deviation of the observed haplotype frequency from the independence expectation. $D = 0$ implies linkage equilibrium; $D \neq 0$ implies disequilibrium.

### Step 4: Normalized Measures

$$
D' = \begin{cases}
\frac{D}{D_{\max}} & \text{if } D \geq 0 \\
\frac{D}{D_{\min}} & \text{if } D < 0
\end{cases}
$$

where $D_{\max} = \min(p_A \cdot p_B', \, p_A' \cdot p_B)$ and $D_{\min} = \max(-p_A \cdot p_B, \, -p_A' \cdot p_B')$.

**Logic:** $D' \in [-1, +1]$; values near $\pm 1$ indicate strong LD; near 0 indicates little LD.

$$
r^2 = \frac{D^2}{p_A \cdot p_B \cdot p_A' \cdot p_B'}
$$

**Logic:** $r^2$ is the squared correlation between two loci, also interpretable as the proportion of variance in one locus explained by the other. Used in GWAS for tag SNP selection (typically $r^2 \geq 0.8$).

### Step 5: Worked Example

Let $p_A = 0.7$, $p_B = 0.4$, $P_{AB} = 0.32$.

First, compute the expected haplotype frequency:

$$
P_{AB}^{\text{expected}} = p_A \cdot p_B = 0.7 \times 0.4 = 0.28
$$

Now compute D:

$$
D = P_{AB} - p_A \cdot p_B = 0.32 - 0.28 = 0.04
$$

Compute $D_{\max}$:

$$
D_{\max} = \min(p_A \cdot p_B', \, p_A' \cdot p_B) = \min(0.7 \times 0.6, \, 0.3 \times 0.4) = \min(0.42, 0.12) = 0.12
$$

Compute $D'$:

$$
D' = \frac{D}{D_{\max}} = \frac{0.04}{0.12} = 0.333
$$

Compute $r^2$:

$$
r^2 = \frac{0.04^2}{0.7 \times 0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.0016}{0.0504} \approx 0.0317
$$

**Interpretation:** Moderate $D'$ (0.333) but low $r^2$ (0.032) — the alleles show historical association but contribute limited mutual predictive power.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE) Test

HWE is a foundational assumption in genetic association studies; deviations can indicate genotyping errors, population stratification, or true selection.

### Step 1: Random Mating Assumption

For a biallelic locus with allele frequencies $p$ and $q$ (where $p + q = 1$), random mating produces the following genotypic frequencies in offspring:

- $AA$ (homozygous major): frequency $p^2$
- $Aa$ (heterozygous): frequency $2pq$
- $aa$ (homozygous minor): frequency $q^2$

### Step 2: Summation Identity

$$
p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = (p + q)^2 = 1^2 = 1
$$

This confirms that the three genotypic classes account for the entire population.

### Step 3: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test

Observed genotype counts $(O_{AA}, O_{Aa}, O_{aa})$ in a sample of size $N$ are compared to expected counts $(E_{AA}, E_{Aa}, E_{aa}) = (N p^2, \, 2Npq, \, Nq^2)$:

$$
\chi^2_{\text{HWE}} = \sum_{i} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
$$

with 1 degree of freedom (since 2 alleles constrain 2 parameters: $N$ and $p$).

**Decision Rule:** Reject HWE if $\chi^2 > 3.84$ (at $\alpha = 0.05$, 1 d.f.).

### Step 4: Worked Example

In a sample of 100 individuals, observed genotypes are: $AA = 60$, $Aa = 30$, $aa = 10$.

Compute allele frequencies:

$$
p = \frac{2(60) + 30}{2(100)} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75, \quad q = 0.25
$$

Expected counts:

$$
E_{AA} = 100 \times 0.75^2 = 56.25
$$
$$
E_{Aa} = 100 \times 2 \times 0.75 \times 0.25 = 37.5
$$
$$
E_{aa} = 100 \times 0.25^2 = 6.25
$$

Compute chi-square:

$$
\chi^2 = \frac{(60-56.25)^2}{56.25} + \frac{(30-37.5)^2}{37.5} + \frac{(10-6.25)^2}{6.25}
$$

$$
\chi^2 = \frac{14.0625}{56.25} + \frac{56.25}{37.5} + \frac{14.0625}{6.25} = 0.25 + 1.5 + 2.25 = 4.0
$$

**Interpretation:** $\chi^2 = 4.0 > 3.84$ → marginal deviation from HWE; warrants further investigation (potential genotyping error in $aa$ calls, or population substructure).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Strategy Selection

| Disease Characteristic | Recommended Strategy | Why |
|------------------------|---------------------|-----|
| Rare, Mendelian, single-gene (e.g., Cystic Fibrosis) | Linkage Analysis | Few families, high penetrance, large effect sizes |
| Common, polygenic, complex (e.g., T2D) | GWAS | Requires large sample sizes, common variants |
| Multiple rare variants, high effect | Whole-Exome Sequencing (WES) | Exonic focus, family-based burden tests |
| Non-coding regulatory variants | Whole-Genome Sequencing (WGS) | Captures regulatory regions |
| Pharmacogenomic trait | Targeted SNP panel + GWAS | Pre-defined gene panel + discovery |
| Somatic mutations (e.g., cancer) | Tumor-normal paired sequencing | Identifies acquired mutations |
| Structural variants (e.g., CNVs) | CGH arrays or long-read sequencing | SNP arrays miss large variants |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Linkage Disequilibrium (LD) and state its role in GWAS.

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | PEEBT866 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer (Structured for 3-mark answer):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Linkage Disequilibrium (LD) refers to the non-random association of alleles at two or more closely linked loci on the same chromosome, where specific allele combinations (haplotypes) occur at frequencies different from those predicted by random assortment. It is mathematically quantified using coefficients **D**, **D'**, and **r²**.

**Role in GWAS (1.5 marks):**
LD is the statistical foundation of GWAS. Because nearby SNPs are correlated due to historical recombination patterns, genotyping a sparse set of "tag SNPs" allows researchers to indirectly capture untyped causal variants. This dramatically reduces genotyping cost while maintaining detection power. Strong LD (high r²) means a single tag SNP can serve as a proxy for many nearby variants within a haplotype block, enabling comprehensive genome-wide surveys with ~500,000 to 2,000,000 markers rather than the >10 million known SNPs.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between parametric and non-parametric linkage analysis.

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | PEEBT866 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer (Tabular format for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Parametric Linkage | Non-Parametric Linkage |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Inheritance Model** | Requires specification (dominant, recessive, X-linked) | No explicit model required |
| **Penetrance** | Must be specified a priori | Not required (model-free) |
| **Disease Allele Frequency** | Must be known/estimated | Not required |
| **Best Suited For** | Mendelian disorders with clear inheritance | Complex diseases, late-onset disorders |
| **Statistical Test** | LOD score, two-point or multi-point | Identity-by-Descent (IBD) sharing, NPL scores |
| **Pedigree Type** | Large, multi-generational families | Affected sib-pairs, nuclear families |
| **Example** | BRCA1, Huntington's disease | Type 1 Diabetes, Schizophrenia |

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Students frequently confuse LD (population-level correlation) with Linkage (family-level co-inheritance). LOD is for linkage; D'/r² is for LD.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 2:** The GWAS significance threshold is p < 5 × 10⁻⁸, not 0.05. Failing to apply Bonferroni correction is a **3-mark deduction**.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 3:** For pedigree drawings in linkage analysis, always label generations (I, II, III) and individual numbers — unlabelled pedigrees receive partial credit only.
> - **Mark Loss Zone 4:** Don't conflate odds ratio (OR) with relative risk (RR); OR is valid for case-control studies. OR = 1 means no association.

---

### OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | PEEBT866 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the Genome-Wide Association Study (GWAS) methodology in detail, including its experimental design, statistical framework, and the importance of multiple testing correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A researcher is investigating the genetic basis of Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) using a GWAS approach. Discuss the challenges encountered in such studies, the role of linkage disequilibrium in SNP selection, and how polygenic risk scores (PRS) are constructed for T2D. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

### Part (a) — GWAS Methodology (7 Marks)

**1. Study Design and Cohort Selection (2 marks):**

GWAS employs a **case-control** design. Thousands of unrelated individuals are classified into:
- **Cases:** Individuals with confirmed disease phenotype (e.g., clinically diagnosed T2D).
- **Controls:** Phenotyped healthy individuals matched for ethnicity, age, and sex.

Sample size is critical — modern GWAS typically include **>100,000 individuals** to detect small effect sizes (OR = 1.05–1.5) that are typical of common diseases.

**2. Genotyping and Imputation (2 marks):**

DNA samples are genotyped on high-density SNP arrays (e.g., Illumina Global Screening Array) that assay **500,000–2,000,000 SNPs** simultaneously. Since typed SNPs are sparse, **imputation** is performed using a reference haplotype panel (e.g., 1000 Genomes, HRC, TOPMed) to statistically infer **>10 million variants** per individual. Imputation leverages LD structure to predict untyped genotypes from observed haplotype patterns.

**3. Statistical Framework (2 marks):**

For each SNP, association is tested using:
- **Allelic chi-square test:** 2×2 contingency table (case allele count vs. control allele count).
- **Logistic regression** (preferred): $\log\left(\frac{P(\text{disease})}{1 - P(\text{disease})}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{SNP} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{covariates}$
- **Cochran-Armitage trend test:** Tests additive genetic models.

**4. Multiple Testing Correction (1 mark):**

With 1,000,000+ tests, the family-wise error rate is controlled using:
- **Bonferroni correction:** $\alpha = 0.05 / 1{,}000{,}000 = 5 \times 10^{-8}$.
- **FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg)** is also used for less stringent criteria.

Results are visualized using a **Manhattan plot** (genomic position on x-axis, −log₁₀ p-value on y-axis), where peaks above the genome-wide significance threshold are considered hits.

---

### Part (b) — Challenges, LD, and PRS in T2D (7 Marks)

**1. Challenges in T2D GWAS (3 marks):**

- **Small Effect Sizes:** Most T2D risk variants have OR < 1.3, requiring massive sample sizes for detection.
- **Locus Heterogeneity:** Over **400 independent T2D-associated loci** identified, with the strongest (TCF7L2, OR ≈ 1.4) explaining only a modest fraction of heritability (the "missing heritability problem").
- **Population Stratification:** Cryptic differences in ancestry between cases and controls can produce false associations. Corrected using **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** and **genomic control** (λ_GC).
- **Phenotypic Heterogeneity:** T2D is defined by hyperglycemia but has diverse etiologies (insulin resistance vs. β-cell dysfunction).
- **Gene-Environment Interactions:** Obesity, diet, and exercise modify genetic risk.

**2. Role of LD in SNP Selection (2 marks):**

The HapMap and 1000 Genomes Projects have mapped LD structure across human populations, revealing that the genome is organized into **haplotype blocks** of 10–100 kb with limited haplotype diversity. T2D GWAS exploits this by selecting **tag SNPs** with $r^2 \geq 0.8$ against untyped causal variants. The TCF7L2 locus, for example, shows extensive LD spanning ~100 kb, allowing a few tag SNPs to capture the association signal. Population-specific LD patterns (e.g., shorter LD in African populations) necessitate ethnically diverse tag SNP panels.

**3. Polygenic Risk Score Construction (2 marks):**

A PRS aggregates the effects of many T2D risk variants into a single quantitative score for each individual:

$$
\text{PRS}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \beta_j \cdot x_{ij}
$$

where $\beta_j$ is the effect size (log-OR) of SNP $j$ from GWAS summary statistics, $x_{ij}$ is the allele count (0, 1, or 2) for individual $i$, and $M$ is the number of SNPs included. Methods like **PRS-CS, LDpred2, and lassosum** use Bayesian shrinkage or penalized regression to weight SNPs by their effect sizes and LD. For T2D, a high PRS (top decile) confers **~3–5× increased risk** compared to the population mean, enabling risk stratification for prevention programs.

---

### OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | PEEBT866 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Discuss the principles of linkage analysis in disease gene mapping. Derive the LOD score formula and explain how it is used to identify disease loci. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast linkage analysis with genome-wide association studies. With suitable examples, explain how Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) approaches have complemented these traditional methods in the genomic era. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

### Part (a) — Linkage Analysis and LOD Score (7 Marks)

**1. Principles of Linkage Analysis (2 marks):**

Linkage analysis is based on **Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment** and the observation that loci on the same chromosome tend to be co-inherited. Two loci are **linked** if they are physically close enough that recombination between them during meiosis is rare. The probability of recombination between two loci is denoted by the **recombination fraction** $\theta$, where:
- $\theta = 0.5$ → unlinked (independent assortment)
- $0 \leq \theta < 0.5$ → linked (lower $\theta$ = tighter linkage)

Linkage analysis tracks the inheritance of **polymorphic genetic markers** (microsatellites, SNPs) and the disease phenotype across family pedigrees. The key assumption is that **recombination is more likely to occur between distant loci** than between close ones. By identifying markers that co-segregate with the disease, the disease gene can be localized to a chromosomal interval.

**2. Derivation of the LOD Score (3 marks):**

**Step 1: Likelihood Formulation**

For a given pedigree with known genotypes at the marker locus and disease status, define two competing hypotheses:
- $H_0$: No linkage, $\theta = 0.5$
- $H_1$: Linkage, $\theta = \theta_0$ (unknown)

The likelihood of observing the pedigree data under each hypothesis is $L(\theta = 0.5)$ and $L(\theta_0)$, respectively.

**Step 2: Likelihood Ratio**

$$
\text{LR}(\theta) = \frac{L(\theta)}{L(0.5)}
$$

**Step 3: Logarithmic Transformation**

Taking the base-10 logarithm yields the LOD score, which is **additive across independent families**:

$$
\text{LOD}(\theta) = \log_{10}\left[\frac{L(\theta)}{L(0.5)}\right]
$$

**Step 4: Multi-Point and Combined LOD**

For multiple pedigrees, total LOD is:

$$
Z_{\text{total}}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i(\theta)
$$

**3. Interpretation and Application (2 marks):**

| LOD Value | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------|
| $\geq 3.0$ | Significant linkage (odds 1000:1) |
| $1.0 - 2.99$ | Suggestive linkage |
| $-2.0$ to $0.99$ | Uninformative |
| $\leq -2.0$ | Excludes linkage at this $\theta$ |

**Application Example:** In Huntington's disease (autosomal dominant), Gusella et al. (1983) used RFLP markers and pedigree LOD analysis to map the HD gene to chromosome 4. The cumulative LOD score exceeded 6, providing overwhelming evidence. Once a locus is identified, the linked interval is refined by **recombination mapping** — affected individuals carrying recombinant chromosomes help narrow the critical region, after which candidate genes are sequenced.

---

### Part (b) — Comparison and NGS Integration (7 Marks)

**1. Linkage vs. GWAS Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Linkage Analysis | GWAS |
|---------|------------------|------|
| **Sample Type** | Multi-generational families, pedigrees | Unrelated case-control individuals |
| **Number of Markers** | Hundreds (microsatellites/SNPs) | Millions (SNP arrays) |
| **Mapping Resolution** | Coarse (~1–10 Mbp) | Fine (~10–500 kbp) |
| **Disease Type** | Rare Mendelian, monogenic | Common, polygenic, complex |
| **Effect Size** | Large (OR > 2) | Small (OR 1.1–1.5) |
| **Statistical Test** | LOD score (parametric) or NPL (non-parametric) | Chi-square, logistic regression |
| **Sample Size** | Few large families | Tens of thousands of individuals |
| **Cost** | Lower per family | High per cohort |
| **Example** | BRCA1 (breast/ovarian cancer) | Type 2 Diabetes, Crohn's disease |

**2. NGS-Enabled Approaches (3 marks):**

Next-Generation Sequencing has revolutionized disease gene mapping by enabling **comprehensive variant detection** at base-pair resolution:

- **Whole-Exome Sequencing (WES):** Captures ~1–2% of the genome encoding all protein-coding exons (~20,000 genes). Effective for identifying rare coding variants underlying Mendelian disorders. Example: **DYNC1H1** mutations in Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease were identified through WES in family trios.
- **Whole-Genome Sequencing (WGS):** Captures the entire ~3.2 Gbp genome, including regulatory and intronic regions. Identifies structural variants, non-coding regulatory mutations, and repeat expansions. Example: **C9orf72** hexanucleotide repeat in ALS/FTD.
- **RNA Sequencing (RNA-seq):** Quantifies gene expression and splicing, enabling **expression QTL (eQTL)** mapping to connect non-coding GWAS hits to functional gene targets.
- **Targeted Panels:** Focused sequencing of pre-selected gene sets (e.g., cancer gene panels with >500 genes) for clinical diagnostics.

**3. Integration Strategy in the Genomic Era (1 mark):**

Modern studies combine approaches: **WES/WGS in multiplex families** for rare variant discovery, **GWAS in large populations** for common variants, and **eQTL/QTL integration** for functional interpretation. Tools like **PLINK, GATK, GEMMA, REGENIE, and SAIGE** provide unified analytical pipelines. The 100,000 Genomes Project (UK) and All of Us (US) exemplify this integrated approach, generating WGS data from hundreds of thousands of participants to map disease genes at unprecedented scale.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Disease Gene Mapping**

### Key Definitions to Memorize
- **Linkage:** Family-based co-inheritance of loci on the same chromosome.
- **Linkage Disequilibrium (LD):** Population-based non-random association of alleles.
- **GWAS:** Genome-Wide Association Study using dense SNP arrays on case-control cohorts.
- **LOD Score:** Base-10 log likelihood ratio favoring linkage; threshold = **3.0**.
- **Haplotype Block:** A chromosomal region of low haplotype diversity bounded by recombination hotspots.
- **PRS:** Polygenic Risk Score — weighted sum of risk alleles from GWAS.

### Critical Numerical Thresholds
- **LOD > 3.0** → significant linkage
- **p < 5 × 10⁻⁸** → GWAS significance
- **r² ≥ 0.8** → tag SNP selection criterion
- **|D'| = 1** → complete LD
- **MAF ≥ 1%** → polymorphism (vs. mutation)

### High-Yield Distinctions (Common Exam Pitfalls)
- **Linkage vs. LD:** Linkage = family/meiosis; LD = population/statistical correlation.
- **Parametric vs. Non-Parametric:** Parametric = specified model; Non-parametric = IBD sharing.
- **Two-point vs. Multi-point LOD:** Two-point = 1 marker; Multi-point = multiple markers simultaneously.
- **HWE Assumption:** Required for valid GWAS; deviations signal genotyping error or stratification.
- **Bonferroni vs. FDR:** Bonferroni is conservative (controls FWER); FDR is less stringent but controls false discovery proportion.

### Essential Formulas (Memorize Before Exam)
- LOD: $\text{LOD}(\theta) = \log_{10}[L(\theta) / L(0.5)]$
- LD: $D = P_{AB} - p_A p_B$
- LD correlation: $r^2 = D^2 / (p_A p_B p_A' p_B')$
- HWE: $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$
- PRS: $\text{PRS}_i = \sum_j \beta_j x_{ij}$
- Bonferroni: $\alpha = 0.05 / N$

### Major Milestones in Disease Gene Mapping (Quick Reference)
- **1983:** HD gene mapped to chromosome 4 (first positional cloning).
- **1990:** CFTR gene identified for Cystic Fibrosis.
- **2003:** Human Genome Project completed (~3.2 Gbp).
- **2005:** HapMap Project launched (LD map of human genome).
- **2007:** First successful GWAS published (Age-related Macular Degeneration, complement factor H).
- **2015+:** 100,000 Genomes and All of Us programs; WGS becomes clinically feasible.
- **2020+:** Polygenic Risk Scores enter clinical trials; population-scale PRS-guided screening.

### Engineering / Medical Applications
- **Pharmacogenomics:** HLA-B*57:01 testing prevents Abacavir hypersensitivity in HIV patients.
- **Companion Diagnostics:** EGFR mutation testing for lung cancer targeted therapy.
- **Prenatal Screening:** Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) for trisomies and monogenic disorders.
- **Cancer Genomics:** Tumor-normal paired WES/WGS identifies driver mutations and therapeutic targets.
- **Predictive Genomics:** BRCA1/2 testing for breast/ovarian cancer risk assessment.

### Key Tools and Databases (Mention by Name in Answers)
- **PLINK** — GWAS and linkage analysis.
- **GATK** — Variant calling from NGS data.
- **HaploReg / RegulomeDB** — Functional annotation of non-coding variants.
- **dbSNP, ClinVar, OMIM** — Variant and disease databases.
- **1000 Genomes, gnomAD** — Population reference panels for imputation and MAF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** When a question asks about *mapping resolution*, remember:
> - Linkage = **megabase scale** (coarse)
> - GWAS = **kilobase scale** (fine)
> - WGS = **base-pair scale** (highest)
> This hierarchy is a frequent short-answer topic.
